title: Refactor build orchestrator to reduce presubmit image footprint and introduce dependency fingerprinting
preview:
Motivation: our presubmit images and toolchain bootstrap time have trended upward as we accumulate optional build helpers and devdeps. This PR refactors the CI build orchestrator to produce a smaller default presubmit image, introduce a dependency fingerprint index for targeted dep-refresh, and add a safe dep-prune pass that can be toggled per-team. The changes aim to reduce CI startup latency, make premerge effects of dependency updates auditable, and lower presubmit I/O on heavily changed branches.

Summary of changes: 1) Introduce 'slim-presubmit' microimage variant that omits optional language SDKs and heavy native toolchains; the orchestrator now selects slim vs full images based on profile tags in the monorepo manifest. 2) Add artifact-dep fingerprinting: CI computes a compact fingerprint of the graph (hash of lockfile + toolchain manifest + changed globs) and writes entries to a small index used to gate dep-refresh. 3) Add an opt-in binary 'dep-pruner' step that runs a dry-run prune and uploads a changelog artifact; when enabled it will annotate PRs with suggested removals. 4) Normalize build DAG generation to reduce duplicate prep steps across language workspaces (reduces parallel container starts). 5) Update presubmit templates and README with how to opt in/out and how to read fingerprint index entries.

Checklist: - [x] Unit tests for fingerprint generation and index storage - [x] E2E smoke for presubmit selection in staging - [x] Add telemetry counters for presubmit image sizes and startup times - [x] Documentation and team opt-in guide - [x] Backwards-compatible default: teams not opting in see no change

Rollout & safety: the new orchestrator respects an opt-in label and a per-team CI profile. We rolled out to the infra and sdk teams for one week, observed ~28% reduction in average presubmit container start time and ~12% fewer packages mounted during preflight. If regressions appear the orchestrator provides a 2-minute fail-open path: set CI_PROFILE=full or add the label ci/force-full-image to a PR.

Notes on motivation vs alternatives: we considered a global forced dep-prune, but that is high risk for large monorepos. Fingerprinting gives a safe gate to only attempt pruning when the fingerprint changes in a way consistent with removal candidates.

Related: ENG-3842 (presubmit-costs), ENG-3910 (dep-curation).

title: Adaptive compaction trigger and batch-aware spill controller
preview:
Summary: This PR introduces two coordinated mechanisms to improve KV cache memory management under mixed workloads: 1) an adaptive compaction trigger that decides when to compact/merge small fragments based on a lightweight online heuristic, and 2) a batch-aware spill controller that paces spill operations according to continuous-batching pressure and kernel utilization.

Motivation: We observed repeated p99 latency spikes during sustained continuous-batching workloads caused by synchronous spill/compaction activity that collided with kernel hot paths. Compaction is beneficial for long-term fragmentation reduction but can cause synchronous work and page movement at inopportune times. The new approach learns a compact/skip decision from running statistics (fragmentation score, eviction pressure, recent page churn) and cooperates with the request scheduler so compaction work is deferred when kernel occupancy is high. Simultaneously, the spill controller uses current batch occupancy and measured kernel utilization to pace background spill bandwidth and avoid head-of-line latency regressions.

Design:
- Adaptive compaction trigger: a lightweight scoring function computed per-kv-shard: F = alpha * frag_score + beta * eviction_pressure + gamma * churn_rate + decay(ewma_access_rate). The coefficients are tunable and default to conservative values. The trigger operates at shard granularity with hysteresis windows to avoid thrash. When the score crosses a threshold, compaction is scheduled as a background job; the scheduler consults runtime.load_signals to delay immediate compaction if avg_kernel_util > kernel_util_threshold or if current_continuous_batch_size > batch_threshold.
- Batch-aware spill controller: spill tasks are stamped with a priority and a pacing token bucket. Tokens are replenished based on measured free_gpu_cycles and desired_background_throughput. If a high-priority request enters the queue and background tokens are low, spill bandwidth is throttled and inexpensive demotion candidates are enqueued instead. The controller exposes per-tenant pacing knobs and a global emergency drain mode that skips pacing when free memory falls below an emergency threshold.
- Integration with scheduler and kernel selection: The schedule path now exposes a "may_defer_background_work" hint so compaction/spill controllers can reduce active background load when kernel-optimized paths are congested. This prevents compaction from preempting GPU kernel work causing p99 regressions.
- Metrics & observability: added metrics kv.compaction.score, kv.compaction.deferred_count, kv.spill.tokens_used, kv.spill.paced_ops, and dashboards to track fragment_score distribution per-tenant.

Implementation notes:
- runtime/kv/compactor.cc/h: new compaction trigger and background compactor worker.
- runtime/kv/spill_controller.cc/h: implements tokenized pacing and per-tenant policies.
- runtime/scheduler/scheduler.cc: exposes workload hints API.
- runtime/kernel_selector.cc: small hook to report kernel occupancy.
- observability/metrics/kv_metrics.md: documents new metrics.
- benchmarks/kv/bench_compaction_latency.yaml: new benchmark scenario.

Backwards compatibility: All behavior behind feature gate "kv.adaptive_compaction_spill_v1" (default off in main). Default behavior unchanged until rollout.

Testing:
- Unit tests: tests/kv/test_compactor_unit.cc, tests/kv/test_spill_controller_unit.cc
- Integration: tests/kv/mixed_rw_continuous_batch_integration.yml simulates sustained batches with background writes
- Stress: nightly bench job added to track p50/p99 token latency under the new controller.

Benchmarks: Synthetic mixed read/write workload (70/30 read/write, seq length 256, warm cache) results:
- Reduced p50 token latency by ~8% at 75% load.
- Reduced p99 token latency spikes by ~14% (fewer synchronous spill events).
- Spill frequency decreased ~36% due to deferred/combined spill windows and better compaction timing.
- Fragmentation score (median per-shard) improved ~22% after 6 hours of simulated traffic.

Rollout plan:
1) Feature flag enabled on internal canary (dedicated-1) with aggressive telemetry.
2) Expand to 10% of dedicated fleets after one week if metrics stable.
3) Global enable for Dedicated customers, then Private with opt-in.
4) Monitor kv.compaction.deferred_count and kv.spill.paced_ops for regressions and abort rollback if p99 increases >5% sustained.

Checklist:
- [x] Feature flag
- [x] Unit tests
- [x] Integration tests
- [x] Benchmarks
- [x] Observability/dashboard
- [x] Docs snippet
- [x] Rollout plan

Related work: ENG-4821 (fragmentation monitoring), ENG-4899 (scheduler hints).

title: Isolated-region protection and safe-fallback with KV-cache drain (hotfix)
preview: Motivation: During a cross-region incident we observed an "islanded" region scenario where the control plane-to-node heartbeats transiently lost egress but local model processes remained healthy. That caused the routing layer to continue directing requests to a region that could not reach shared KV/cache invalidation, resulting in stale prefix reuse and increased error rates for model variants dependent on external context. This PR implements a detection-and-protect hotfix: (1) islanded region detector with conservative hysteresis, (2) a 'safe-fallback' routing policy that redirects new requests to healthy regions while letting in-flight streams finish, and (3) a KV-cache drain path for the local runtime to evict prefix/KV entries that could become stale when the region is isolated. The change is intentionally additive and guarded by an operator-configurable feature flag. Summary of changes: added coordinator/region_health.go for island detection and propagation of islanded state to the router, implemented runtime/cache/kv_drain.go which exposes a non-blocking KV drain hook used during safe-fallback transitions, updated runtime/router to consult the islanded state and trigger steady-fallback instead of immediate hard cutover, added Prometheus alert rules and dashboards to observability/alerts/prometheus_rules.yml, added an operational runbook docs/operational-runbooks/islanded-region.md, added e2e test e2e-tests/region-failover.test.ts that simulates control plane egress loss, and updated helm chart default(s) to keep the feature flag off by default. Commits: ["b8c3f4b - coordinator: region health and islanded state propagation","2f9d020 - runtime: non-blocking KV-cache drain hook and drain worker","43e7a12 - router: safe-fallback policy and steady handoff logic","a1b2c3d - observability: islanded-region alerting and dashboard tweaks","d4e5f6a - docs: runbook for islanded-region response","f7a8b9c - tests: e2e simulating islanded region and reroute behaviour"]. Files changed (high level): ["coordinator/region_health.go","runtime/cache/kv_drain.go","runtime/router/router.go","observability/prometheus_rules.yml","docs/operational-runbooks/islanded-region.md","e2e-tests/region-failover.test.ts","charts/redwood/values.yaml","pkg/flags/feature_flags.go"].

title: introduce-stage-aware-ci-fanout-and-dependency-compactifier
preview:
Motivation: feedback from large teams and recurring release friction showed two pain points: (1) presubmit lanes run too many linear checks for small changes, increasing latency and contributor churn; (2) devdeps and monorepo surface area have accumulated transitive baggage making CI images and cache warm slower. This PR introduces a stage-aware CI fanout orchestrator and a dependency compactifier that trims dev-only transitive deps from CI images and lockfile footprints. Goals: reduce median premerge turnaround by ~30%, shrink CI image build time, and make dependency updates less noisy. Summary of changes:
- Add ci/fanout-controller: lightweight orchestrator that maps PRs to targeted premerge lanes based on path heuristics and changed module manifests. Controls fanout degree per-stage (lint/unit/integration) and supports fast-fail propagation.
- Introduce tooling/dep-compactifier: Go-based tool that analyses package manifests, dev vs prod usage, and produces a slimmed devlock plus optional dev-only manifest for local dev. Integrates with existing lockfile pipeline as an opt-in gate.
- Rework existing workflows: split the monolithic premerge job into staged runners (fast-lint, infra-filter, unit-shard, integration-smoke). Add annotations for shard affinity and cache warm hints.
- Replace several bash helper scripts with a Go entrypoint to reduce CI shell complexity and allow deterministic exit codes.
- Document lightweight-presubmit image and add a CI job to build and publish the slim image used for fast lanes.
Transition notes: the fanout-controller is feature-flagged (env CI_FANOUT_ENABLED=false by default) and the dep-compactifier runs behind a Linear opt-in label to avoid unexpected lock churn. Runbook and rollback procedure are included in the description and docs files.
Performance and correctness: initial benchmark on a sample of 350 PRs shows median premerge latency drop from 14m -> 9m (35% reduction) for small doc/typings changes. Integration coverage is preserved by ensuring the orchestrator always routes to the integration-smoke stage for cross-module changes.
Risks: dep-compactifier could produce a too-aggressive devlock in edge cases (native-build dependency graphs). Mitigations add a conservative mode and a charlie-run approval gate.

title: Introduce batch-affinity optimizer and 'quant-sentinel' graceful quantization profile
preview: Motivation: We have recurring tenant noise and suboptimal packing when multiple small requests with similar token shapes hit the same serving pool. Current batching is agnostic to tenant affinity which causes more KV cache misses and inefficient GPU utilization. Additionally, aggressive quantization rollouts have caused occasional quality regressions for tail workloads. This PR introduces two coordinated features: (1) a batch-affinity optimizer that prefers packing requests with high locality (tenant, route, prompt-shape signatures) while respecting latency SLOs and (2) a conservative 'quant-sentinel' quantization profile that progressively reduces precision by lane and includes a rollback sentinel that forces fallback to a safe quantized variant on quality regressions. Design/changes summary: - Routing/packing: new affinity scoring module (affinity_score) that computes a lightweight locality score using tenant_id, model_version, prefix hash, and token-length bucket. The packer will bias towards high-affinity groups while still respecting pack size and latency headroom. - Prefill/cache: added an affinity-aware prefill hint that enables soft KV cache pinning for high-affinity lanes (TTL-based, configurable per-tenant). - Quantization: added a new profile 'quant-sentinel' that annotates model variants with a sentinel threshold and a staged degradation path: fp16 -> int8-symmetric -> int8-symmetric+kv-compress. Each stage has quality gates and automatic fallback to a verified baseline when regressions are detected. - Telemetry/metrics: new metrics (batch_affinity_ratio, affinity_pack_efficiency, quant_sentinel_fallbacks) and tracing tags (affinity_group_id, quant_stage) for post-deploy analysis. - CLI/tooling: unit-econ dry-run flag to 'redwood-econ' CLI to simulate per-tenant cost delta with current traffic shape and proposed affinities/quant profiles. Rollout plan: 1) Canary deploy optimizer into one region with 5% traffic for non-enterprise tenants for 48 hours. 2) Collect metrics and run quality probes (eval set + live A/B) using the quant sentinel gate. 3) Ramp to 50% with telemetry gates, then to 100% if no regressions. Checklist: - [x] affinity scoring unit tests - [x] packer integration tests with latency SLOs - [x] quant-sentinel unit tests and sentinel regression hooks - [x] telemetry dashboards (draft) - [x] CLI dry-run implemented - [x] e2e smoke test in benchmark-lab Observability notes: initial smoke benchmarks show a 9-12% token/sec utilization increase for mixed small-request workloads and a 7-9% median latency decrease for 0-128 token requests under load. Quality gate simulated on our eval set shows <0.5% delta for the first quant stage, and 1.5-2.1% for stage 2 for large-context prompts (above 1024 tokens) — hence the sentinel fallback behavior. Motivation references: ENG-1892 (tenant packing problems), ENG-2041 (quant rollout safe paths), SUP-4123 (customer incident showing regression during quant rollout).

title: compat: strict media negotiation + per-file ingestion-rate-limits for tool attachments
preview:
Context: Our OpenAI-compat layer has had recurring issues when clients upload ambiguous or binary attachments without clear content-type or length headers. These cause parsing retries, confused streaming boundaries, and occasional function_call failures when tool runtimes receive corrupted payloads. This PR introduces two complementary protections: (1) deterministic media negotiation and normalization so the compat layer always annotates attachments with a canonical content-type + checksum; (2) per-file ingestion rate limits and adaptive chunk checks to prevent slow/bursty clients from overwhelming streaming parsers and downstream tool runtimes.

What this changes (high level):
- Compat layer now enforces an Accept/Content-Type negotiation step for incoming attachments. Preferred content-types for tool calls are application/json (pointer/meta), application/octet-stream (binary payloads), and multipart/form-data for multi-part uploads. Ambiguous cases (e.g. text/plain without charset for likely binary data) are rejected with a 400 and a clear error that lists accepted types.
- Attachments without a declared content-type will trigger a lightweight sniff: if first 512 bytes look like UTF-8 JSON/text we label application/json or text/plain; otherwise we label application/octet-stream. Sniffing is deterministic and logged for audits.
- Each file stream now carries an enforced per-file ingress rate limit (configurable per-customer and per-route). By default set to 4 MB/s for hosted API and 8 MB/s for Dedicated. Exceeding the rate returns a 429 with Retry-After and a hint header (X-File-Rate-Limit).
- For streaming uploads we added a small windowing protocol: the compat proxy tolerates out-of-order chunk arrival within a 500ms reorder window, but rejects incremental chunks that lack proper boundary markers when rate limits are tripped. This significantly reduces repeated partial-parsing in the serving runtime.
- For function/tool calls that reference files, we now require file metadata (content_type, size, sha256) in the function_call arguments. If missing, the compat layer computes sha256 and injects metadata before handing to the tool runtime. This prevents tool runtimes from having to re-read streams and reduces platform I/O.

Why this is safe and backward compatible:
- Well-formed clients that already send content-type and standard multipart/form-data will be unaffected.
- Clients that relied on opaque coercion of text/plain->binary will now get a clear error and guidance to set Content-Type or wrap the file as base64 in JSON. The error message includes a one-line migration hint to keep developer friction low.

Tests & validation included:
- Unit tests for negotiation logic, sniff heuristics, and sha256 injection.
- Integration tests that exercise multipart uploads, chunked transfer-encoding streams, and slow-client scenarios using our perf-canary harness.
- End-to-end tests: function_call with file refs across redwood (compat) -> runtime -> tool-invoker. Added a compatibility matrix test that runs against a matrix of SDK versions (python:1.x/2.x, ts:1.x) to ensure we don't regress client expectations.

Checklist: 
- [x] Implement negotiation + sniffing code in compat proxy
- [x] Add per-file rate-limiter middleware with config knobs on route/customer level
- [x] Compute and inject file metadata (sha256, content_type) for function_call refs
- [x] Add logging + audit events for sniffing decisions
- [x] Add unit/integration/e2e tests
- [x] Update docs/compat-guide (short migration blurb)

Related: ENG-2330 (rate limiting webhooks influenced design), PM-411 (docs/migration text), SUP-981 (customer incident that motivated stricter errors).
text:
Context: Our OpenAI-compat layer has had recurring issues when clients upload ambiguous or binary attachments without clear content-type or length headers. These cause parsing retries, confused streaming boundaries, and occasional function_call failures when tool runtimes receive corrupted payloads. This PR introduces two complementary protections: (1) deterministic media negotiation and normalization so the compat layer always annotates attachments with a canonical content-type + checksum; (2) per-file ingestion rate limits and adaptive chunk checks to prevent slow/bursty clients from overwhelming streaming parsers and downstream tool runtimes.

What this changes (high level):
- Compat layer now enforces an Accept/Content-Type negotiation step for incoming attachments. Preferred content-types for tool calls are application/json (pointer/meta), application/octet-stream (binary payloads), and multipart/form-data for multi-part uploads. Ambiguous cases (e.g. text/plain without charset for likely binary data) are rejected with a 400 and a clear error that lists accepted types.
- Attachments without a declared content-type will trigger a lightweight sniff: if first 512 bytes look like UTF-8 JSON/text we label application/json or text/plain; otherwise we label application/octet-stream. Sniffing is deterministic and logged for audits.
- Each file stream now carries an enforced per-file ingress rate limit (configurable per-customer and per-route). By default set to 4 MB/s for hosted API and 8 MB/s for Dedicated. Exceeding the rate returns a 429 with Retry-After and a hint header (X-File-Rate-Limit).
- For streaming uploads we added a small windowing protocol: the compat proxy tolerates out-of-order chunk arrival within a 500ms reorder window, but rejects incremental chunks that lack proper boundary markers when rate limits are tripped. This significantly reduces repeated partial-parsing in the serving runtime.
- For function/tool calls that reference files, we now require file metadata (content_type, size, sha256) in the function_call arguments. If missing, the compat layer computes sha256 and injects metadata before handing to the tool runtime. This prevents tool runtimes from having to re-read streams and reduces platform I/O.

Why this is safe and backward compatible:
- Well-formed clients that already send content-type and standard multipart/form-data will be unaffected.
- Clients that relied on opaque coercion of text/plain->binary will now get a clear error and guidance to set Content-Type or wrap the file as base64 in JSON. The error message includes a one-line migration hint to keep developer friction low.

Tests & validation included:
- Unit tests for negotiation logic, sniff heuristics, and sha256 injection.
- Integration tests that exercise multipart uploads, chunked transfer-encoding streams, and slow-client scenarios using our perf-canary harness.
- End-to-end tests: function_call with file refs across redwood (compat) -> runtime -> tool-invoker. Added a compatibility matrix test that runs against a matrix of SDK versions (python:1.x/2.x, ts:1.x) to ensure we don't regress client expectations.

Checklist: 
- [x] Implement negotiation + sniffing code in compat proxy
- [x] Add per-file rate-limiter middleware with config knobs on route/customer level
- [x] Compute and inject file metadata (sha256, content_type) for function_call refs
- [x] Add logging + audit events for sniffing decisions
- [x] Add unit/integration/e2e tests
- [x] Update docs/compat-guide (short migration blurb)

Related: ENG-2330 (rate limiting webhooks influenced design), PM-411 (docs/migration text), SUP-981 (customer incident that motivated stricter errors).

title: v1/chat: stabilize streaming handshake; enforce stop-token precedence and introduce role-alias mapping
preview: Context: Customers integrating via the OpenAI /v1/chat.completions surface have seen two classes of issues in production: 1) transient stream disconnects during tool/function call handoffs (client resumes see inconsistent framing), and 2) ambiguity in how stop tokens are applied when stop arrays are provided alongside role-level stop hints or tool-generated token sequences. This PR unifies several compatibility behaviors to reduce surprise for downstream clients and to harden streaming handshakes during function/tool call routing. Motivation: Provide predictable, backward-compatible semantics for v1 chat parity while reducing the failure surface during streaming toolcalls. Goals: (a) stabilize the streaming handshake so that tool/function call initiation and the subsequent resumption of the assistant stream never produce overlapping or dropped deltas, (b) define and implement a deterministic stop-token precedence order (explicit request param > tool-produced stop sequence > role-level hints), (c) add an opt-in role_alias mapping so customers sending alternative role names (e.g., "assistant_bot", "system_agent") map to canonical OpenAI roles for compatibility, and (d) wrap logprobs and structured scores in a deterministic envelope that matches OpenAI parity expectations when streaming deltas are emitted. Changes: - runtime/stream: implement a two-phase handshake for toolcall handoff with explicit ACK/FIN markers and idempotent resume tokens so retries do not duplicate tokens - api/openai_compat: introduce a stop precedence resolver that accepts stop arrays and role/tool hints and returns the canonical stop set applied to generation - api/openai_compat: add role_alias config parsing and mapping; default mappings included and overridable via server config - models/compat: attach a small envelope to streaming delta messages: choices[].delta.{content,role,tool_call,logprobs} to avoid ambiguous flattened fields - sdk/python: update OpenAI-compat adapter to accept role_alias and expose resolved_stop in response metadata - tests: new integration tests exercising stream resume, stop precedence and role aliasing Backwards compatibility and rollout: default behavior is backward-compatible; the stop precedence policy is additive and only changes behavior when multiple stop sources are present (rare). Role-alias mapping is opt-in per-tenant via configuration flag; we set sane defaults for common aliases. The streaming handshake change is released behind a runtime feature-flag; feature is enabled for canary tenants and then rolled out to all after 72h of telemetry. Checklist: [x] unit tests for precedence resolver [x] integration test for stream resume [x] SDK adapter updates [x] e2e smoke in Canary [x] docs note in openai/v1_chat.md Testing: CI includes unit and integration runs. Canary telemetry evaluated for token duplication and error rates for 72h. Observability: added new metrics stream.handshake_retries, stream.duplicate_delta_count, compat.role_alias_mappings_applied Related: ENG-1542 (stream stability), ENG-1588 (openai compat stop semantics), SUP-4321 customer report Refs: docs/openai/v1_chat.md, runtime/stream/README.md

title: quarantine-sse-chunks-and-delimiter-healing-for-partial-json
preview:
Context and motivation: We observed intermittent client-side crashes and invalid JSON errors when streaming long chat responses through the OpenAI-compat SSE adapter. Root cause: a mix of small TCP packet boundaries, server-side aggressive chunking, and occasional delimiter bytes embedded in model token escapes resulted in mid-JSON fragments being emitted without clear boundaries. Prior fixes focused on delimiting heuristics but still allowed tight races where a fragment split removed the terminal '}' or a tool-call sentinel.

This PR implements a two-part strategy: (1) a quarantining window for suspect fragments so the parser doesn't emit partial JSON to higher layers until it has greater confidence; (2) a delimiter-healing state machine that uses lightweight delimiter fingerprints, sequence-checkpoint hints, and backoff/hedging to reconstruct or request missing spans where possible. Together these reduce spurious parse errors, avoid delivering truncated structured outputs to callers, and maintain OpenAI-compat behavior for function/tool calling.

What changed (high level):
- Introduced pkg/sse/healer.go: quarantine buffer, delimiter fingerprinting, and a non-blocking repair loop that attempts local reassembly before escalating to a retry/hedge policy.
- Parser changes in pkg/sse/parser.go: tag suspicious chunks with a quarantine flag, defer JSON emission until healer signals a boundary-safe span. Added hooks for seq checkpoints emitted by the runtime.
- Compatibility adapter update in api/openai/compat/streaming_adapter.go: preserve original event ordering and ensure function-call sentinel handoff is only exposed after heal/commit.
- New unit and fuzz tests in pkg/sse/healer_test.go covering: escaped delimiter collisions, UTF-8 split across frames, and synthetic out-of-order fragment arrivals.
- Added an e2e scenario harness tests/e2e/sse_partial_json.yaml to reproduce previous customer-visible failure.
- Documentation: docs/streaming-framing.md expands the guidance on quarantine behavior and rollout knobs.

Implementation notes and heuristics:
- Quarantine window is size-bounded (config default 4KB, configurable per-route) and time-bounded (default 500ms). Suspicious fragments are identified by checksum mismatch of delimiter fingerprint and by trailing-token heuristics (incomplete UTF-8, unbalanced braces, dangling tool-call sentinel).
- Delimiter fingerprint: a tiny canonicalization of trailing bytes and escape context to detect fragmented delimiters without maintaining full tokenization state. This allows cheap detection and grouping of fragments that likely belong to the same logical JSON span.
- Healing flow: local coalescing -> fingerprint match -> emit; if local coalescing fails within the window we fall back to issuing a hedged request for the missing tail from the model-serving layer (configurable; default off in hosted to avoid duplicate billing).
- Backpressure: create a soft-fence that slows downstream delivery if quarantine occupancy grows; integrates with scheduler to prioritize completing suspect spans.

Testing and verification:
- Unit tests added for all heuristics and edge cases. Fuzz harness included to stress UTF-8 splits and delimiter collisions.
- E2E harness reproduces the customer failure vector and shows the new parser reduces invalid-JSON delivery rate from 0.9% to <0.01% under synthetic congestion.
- CI: lint + unit + e2e (the e2e job is gated behind an opt-in label in CI to keep default runs fast).

Rollout and compatibility:
- Feature gated behind experimental flag 'sse_quarantine_healer' controlled at route-level in the config. Default: off for non-Redwood dedicated/private until a quiet period of 72 hours in canary.
- No breaking API changes. The adapter preserves OpenAI stream semantics but delays commit of structured outputs until the fragment is healed; client-visible behavior only differs by fewer parse errors and a bounded (~500ms) potential additional delay when quarantine heuristics trigger.

Checklist:
- [x] Parser q/o quarantine flag
- [x] Healer state machine + unit tests
- [x] Integration tests for function-call sentinel ordering
- [x] Fuzz tests for UTF-8 and delimiter collisions
- [x] Docs update and rollout notes
- [x] CI green (with opt-in e2e)

Notes and follow-ups:
- Instrumentation: added metrics sse.quarantine.size, sse.heal.attempts, sse.heal.success_rate for the console. Follow-up: add trace tags to token-level traces for postmortems (ENG-4527).
- Consider exposing a per-tenant hedging opt-in for Dedicated customers; this is tracked as ENG-4579.

Related work: references prior SSE fixes but takes a different approach by quarantining ambiguous fragments instead of immediate delimiter-normalization. This is complementary to the checksum/seq approaches in earlier PRs and reduces noisy false-positives in structured output parsing.

title: credential-sanity-linter-and-sdk-interop-polish
preview:
Motivation: Teams keep hitting subtle auth edge-cases when swapping credential providers across SDKs (Python/TypeScript/Go). This patch introduces a credential-sanity linter, cross-SDK interop smoke tests, and a small set of streaming API ergonomics fixes and version bumps. The goal is to reduce incidents caused by mis-typed credential payloads and to make streaming helpers more uniform across languages. Summary of changes below with checklists and upgrade guidance.

What this PR does: Adds a new repo-level tool (tools/cred-lint) that validates credential payloads and common misconfigurations used in our SDK tests; standardizes streaming helper names and optional parameters across sdks/; adds interop smoke tests that call the same scripted scenarios from each SDK variant; bumps SDK packaged versions to pick up ergonomics helpers; fixes a handful of flaky reauth races visible in CI.

Checklist: [x] Add cred-lint CLI + JSON schema (tools/cred-lint) [x] Wire cred-lint into CI smoke-test job [x] Add interop smoke tests (tests/interop/) which run Py/TS/Go snippets against local test harness [x] Rename streaming helpers for parity (py: StreamReader->StreamHandle, ts: streamReader->streamHandle, go: StreamReader->StreamHandle) [x] Small API ergonomics: optional ctx param made last arg in all three SDKs [x] Patch flaky reauth path and telemetry hooks [x] Bump SDK package metadata (python v1.15.0, typescript v1.27.0, go v1.8.1) [x] Update docs snippets and quickstarts

Migration notes: Downstream users who relied on positional arg order for stream helpers will need to update call sites where an explicit context/opts object is supplied. The change is backwards-compatible for keyword/explicit arg usage; the linter will surface common cases and the SDK release notes include quick diffs.

Files of interest: tools/cred-lint/schema.json, tools/cred-lint/cli.py, sdks/python/redwood/stream.py, sdks/python/setup.cfg, sdks/typescript/src/stream.ts, sdks/typescript/package.json, sdks/go/stream.go, sdks/go/go.mod, tests/interop/python_scenario.py, tests/interop/ts_scenario.ts, tests/interop/go_scenario.go, ci/smoke-interoperability.yaml, docs/sdk-migration/stream-rename.md.

Related: ENG-1129 (reduce auth incidents), ENG-1144 (sdk ergonomics parity), SUP-872 (customer-reported streaming auth race).

title: Longtail SLO meter and annotation adapter
preview: Motivation: We have seen sporadic, low-volume prompt-set failures that escape per-run thresholds but aggregate into customer-visible issues over time (long tail). This PR implements a Longtail SLO Meter that maintains a decay-weighted time series of failure signals and a companion Annotation Adapter to attach contextual evidence (examples, model variant, env) to flagged prompt-sets. The goal is to surface gradual SLO drift earlier and enable actionable handoff to triage workflows without increasing noisy CI failures. Design summary: 1) longtail_meter module: computes an exponentially decayed aggregate for each prompt-cohort keyed by prompt_set_id + model_variant, supports configurable half-life and bucket lengths; 2) annotation_adapter: lightweight pluggable interface (sync+async) that writes structured evidence to eval metadata and an optional annotations store; 3) ci-integration: a CI soft-fail mode that transitions from local eval failure -> sustained longtail deviation -> automatic Linear ticket creation with pre-filled evidence. Backwards compatibility: default behavior is opt-in via eval config. No changes to existing eval schema unless the annotation_adapter is enabled. Config knobs: decay_half_life (minutes/hours/days), min_sample_threshold, sustained_window_count, alert_threshold. Testing: unit tests for aggregator math, integration tests with synthetic prompt-sets, end-to-end smoke run against nightly eval harness. Rollout plan: gated behind feature flag and gradual enabling for internal teams before rolling to public CI gating. Related work: ENG-4287 (slo instrumentation), ENG-4301 (annotation store RFC).

title: Lockstep SSE windowing and function-call handoff to prevent partial JSON emission
preview:
Context: Intermittent user reports and internal load tests showed that long-lived SSE connections occasionally emitted fragments that, when consumed by OpenAI-compatible clients, produced partial JSON objects for function/tool call payloads. Root cause: the previous coalescing strategy favored throughput by merging fragments eagerly, and the streaming parser could flush partial JSON tokens when a function-call sentinel spanned a fragmentation boundary. This caused downstream consumers that assume atomic JSON frames to throw parse errors and led to retries and duplicated tool calls.

Motivation: We need a conservative, low-risk change to the SSE emission pipeline that prevents partial JSON emission for tool/function call boundaries while preserving overall throughput and backpressure characteristics. This PR implements a lockstep windowing approach: event fragments that may contain function-call sentinels are fenced with a small serialization handshake so reassembly is deterministic and ordering is preserved across retries and reconnects.

What this change does (high level):
- Introduces a lightweight window-state machine in runtime/sse/coalescer.go that tracks event edges and defers flush when a window contains a potential function-call boundary.
- Adds a serialization fence token ("--rf") that is omitted from the delivered SSE payload but used internally to ensure coalescer and parser agree on flush points. The fence is never exposed in the public API or to users.
- Enhances the OpenAI-compat stream parser to treat the fence as a synchronization marker: when a fence is present, the parser holds partial JSON until the matching fence/flush pair is seen.
- Adds targeted unit and integration tests that simulate interleaved tokens, reconnects, and partial-delivery scenarios.
- Provides a gradual rollout plan: feature-flagged on the coalescer and enabled by default for main, with an opt-out for Dedicated/Private customers if required for compatibility.

Checklist:
- [x] Design review with runtime and api teams (ENG-9142)
- [x] Unit tests covering edge cases (fragment split in UTF-8 boundary, split within string, split inside function-call args)
- [x] Integration test against openai-compat parser harness
- [x] E2E load test demonstrating <5% p99 token latency regression under typical traffic (verified)
- [x] Documentation PR (docs link) for rollout notes and rollback steps

Backward compatibility: The internal fence token is never emitted to clients and the OpenAI-compatible streaming endpoint continues to present the same SSE wire format. Consumers that already tolerate partial tokens will be unaffected; consumers that previously observed partial JSON parse errors should see fewer failures.

Rollout notes: Feature gated for 72 hours with monitoring dashboards in Console (SSE-parse-errors, toolcall-retries). If any significant increase in latency or user-visible regressions occur, we will toggle the flag and revert the change.

Related: ENG-9142 (coalescer redesign), ENG-9299 (openai-compat stability), SUP-445 (support ticket capturing production incidents).

title: align openai /v1/chat.completions role semantics, stop/logprobs and structured-response framing
preview: Motivation: Customers expect exact parity with OpenAI's /v1/chat.completions for role handling, stop sequences, and logprobs output shape. We also have recurring streaming edgecases where role tokens arrive as separate SSE events leading to downstream SDKs mis-attributing first assistant delta. This PR harmonizes the OpenAI-compat layer to produce: 1) canonical role deltas where the initial assistant role is coalesced into the first token event; 2) explicit mapping for 'stop' as either string or array and consistent enforcement across server- and client-side truncation; 3) support for returning token-level logprobs and top_k entries when logprobs>0 is requested; 4) a structured-output framing option that injects a stable JSON wrapper event and schema hint to help SDKs auto-parse structured responses. Changes are backward-compatible by default: legacy clients keep previous behavior unless the compatibility flag openai_compat.enable_new_role_delta is enabled in config; the structured-output framing is opt-in via parameter structured_output=true. Risk/compatibility notes: the logprobs payload can be large and is gated behind both the request param and a per-tenant quota to avoid accidental cost spikes. Streaming changes required subtle buffering to coalesce role tokens; buffer size is capped and falls back to previous behavior on overflow to avoid head-of-line blocking. What this PR does: -- Introduces openai_compat role coalescing logic and deterministic SSE event order. -- Adds parsing and normalization for stop param (string|array) and validates server truncation semantics. -- Implements token-level logprobs serialization matching OpenAI shape: tokens, token_logprobs, top_logprobs (top-k as objects). -- Adds structured-output framing: emits an initial "structured_schema" SSE event with user-provided schema hint, then emits 'delta' events with content_parts tagged. -- Adds comprehensive unit and integration tests, plus an e2e replay harness against a testrpc model to validate SSE order. Testing: new unit tests in pkg/openai_compat/*, integration tests in tests/integration/openai_compat_streaming_test.go, and end-to-end recorded replay scenarios added to benchmark-lab. CI: initial flaky test in sse_emitter was fixed by adding a small deterministic backoff in the emitter; all checks green on final run. Docs: updated docs/openai_compat.md and examples with structured-output example. Rollout plan: feature gated, enabled for staged tenants during canary week; monitoring added to detect a 5xx or SSE reorder regression. Checklist: [x] role coalescing logic implemented [x] stop param normalization [x] logprobs serialization [x] structured-output framing (opt-in) [x] unit/integration/e2e tests added [x] docs updated [x] feature flag and monitoring added

title: manifest-diff-builder and KMS-stamped attestations for incremental private-installer releases
preview: Motivation: make private-installer releases incremental and cryptographically auditable without re-bundling full artifacts for every patch. This PR implements a manifest-diff builder along with KMS-stamped attestations for every delta to enable fast rollforward/rollback and compact provenance storage. Summary: add a new release tool 'manifest-diff' that computes a deterministic delta index between two installer builds, produce a compact delta tarball and an accompanying attest.json signed via customer-configured KMS (supports AWS KMS and Vault HSM). The pipeline integrates the diff artifacts into the release graph and updates release-manifests.yaml with delta entries and signature metadata. Commits summary: 1) initial manifest-diff implementation and tests; 2) KMS attestation client and integration layer; 3) CI pipeline changes to upload delta artifacts to release-bucket and verify attestations in staged job; 4) docs and upgrade notes; 5) small fixes for idempotent replay and key rotation handling. Files changed (high level): tools/manifest-diff/main.go added; tools/manifest-diff/README.md added; internal/release/delta_index.go added; internal/release/attest_kms.go added; ci/pipelines/release-delta.yml modified; charts/installer/values.yaml bumped for delta-recovery hooks; docs/private-installer/release-delta.md added; tests/fixtures/deltas/* added; scripts/verify-attest.sh added. Small illustrative snippet: delta_index entries are structured as {"from":"sha256:...","to":"sha256:...","files":[{"path":"charts/installer.tgz","type":"tar","digest":"sha256:...","size":12345}],"attests":[{"kind":"kms","uri":"awskms://arn:aws:kms:...","sig":"base64..."}]} Highlights and decisions: 1) Use KMS-stamped attestations rather than embedding private keys in CI; CI signs the attestation blob via customer-supplied KMS credential or a staging HSM; 2) Deltas are additive by file path and deterministic by sorted manifest entries to ensure stable signatures; 3) Release manifest schema extended with 'delta_index' block and backward-compatible fields; 4) Added verification step to release pipeline to fail if attestation verification fails. Testing: unit tests for delta computation and attestation verification, integration run against a synthetic release pair, at-scale smoke run on ci-stage with 3 sample deltas. Backwards compat: release consumers that do not understand delta entries will continue to use full artifacts; new clients can fetch and apply deltas. Rollout plan: merge to main, enable opt-in delta builds behind a feature flag in release pipeline for two weeks, run mirror promotion to ensure consumers can fall back to full artifacts. Follow-ups: add in-toto statement mapping and OCI registry push for delta tarballs (ENG-3378), add Vault auto-rotation support. Review thread summary: Bethany Li requested explicit handling for KMS key rotation and a replay protection counter in attestations; author implemented 'key_meta' and 'replay_nonce' fields and added tests. Rishi asked for stronger CI gating; pipeline now has attestation-verify job that runs before artifact-promotion and is green on ci-stage. Diego noted performance concerns for large releases; manifest-diff now streams file lists and avoids loading full file contents into memory. Approvals: Rishi Patel approved after CI green and dex fixes; Bethany Li approved after key rotation tests; Diego Morales approved after small performance tweak. Merge: squashed and merged to main via squash-merge. Release notes: "Adds manifest-diff builder and KMS-stamped attestations enabling incremental private-installer releases and compact provenance. Delta artifacts are optional and gated behind release flag." Associated follow-ups: ENG-3378 for OCI delta registry, ENG-3421 to add Vault auto-rotation. CI Summary: initial lint failed on goimports, fixed in commit 3; one flaky integration test retried and passed; final ci-status is pass. Merge metadata: method=squash, sha=abcd1234efgh, merged_by=Rishi Patel. Additional operational notes: operators should add allowed KMS principals to release pipeline role and rotate keys using the documented key_meta guidelines; a migration script to backfill delta_index for recent 5 releases is included in scripts/backfill-deltas.sh.

title: Resumable streaming: disconnect quarantine, server timeouts, and function-call idempotency
preview:
Context and motivation: Production customers hit a class of errors where brief client disconnects (mobile network flaps, browser tab swaps) caused long-running streaming requests that invoked tools to either get duplicated or be lost after client reconnect. Existing resume tokens preserved raw streaming offsets but did not maintain function-call intent or partial outputs, making resuming tool invocations unsafe. This PR introduces a small set of changes to make streaming resumes predictable and safe for function/tool calls and structured output in the OpenAI compatibility layer.

What this change does (single continuous description): Adds a server-side "reconnect quarantine" (default 20s) during which a session can reconnect and claim in-flight stream state; introduces a server-enforced soft-cancel TTL for tool/function calls that allows the runtime to decide whether to allow the tool to finish or abort early when a client disconnects; augments resume tokens with a per-invocation idempotency key and partial-result checkpoint so a resumed session can continue or safely re-run a tool without duplicating side effects; updates the OpenAI-compat adapter to carry function_call metadata across resume and to map resumed partial payloads into the structured output contract; updates JS/Python SDKs to surface resume semantics, honor reconnect hints, and provide explicit opt-out flags for aggressive hard-cancel; adds end-to-end harness tests that simulate flaky disconnects and targeted regression coverage for previously observed duplicate-tool invocation failures.

Checklist: - [x] Design doc and linear: ENG-3881 - [x] Unit + integration tests - [x] E2E reconnect harness with simulated network flakiness - [x] Backwards-compatible adapter changes with feature flag (compat.resume_v2) - [x] Docs and SDK changelog entries - [x] Perf regression bench run (no regression in p50 latency observed)

Commits (high-level): - "session: introduce reconnect_quarantine manager with TTL and reclaim logic" - "runtime: soft-cancel policy and invocation idempotency keys" - "openai_adapter: preserve function_call and partial_args across resume" - "sdk-js: expose resumeToken behavior & reconnectHints to clients" - "sdk-py: resume client + test harness" - "tests: e2e flaky disconnect harness and regression cases" - "docs: streaming resume semantics and migration guidance"

Changed areas (high level): service/streaming/session_manager.go - new reconnect quarantine manager; runtime/invocation/soft_cancel.go - soft-cancel/safe-retry logic; adapters/openai/compat.go - carry function_call metadata and partial-result checkpointing; sdk/js/streaming.ts - resumeToken handling, reconnect hints; sdk/python/stream_client.py - same for python SDK; tests/e2e/stream_reconnect_spec.py - new harness; docs/api/streaming_resume.md - documentation and migration notes; console/observability/alerts.yaml - tuned alert thresholds for streaming disconnect spikes.

Review conversation (highlights): - Priya: "Can you clarify how the idempotency key is generated and where it is stored? If it's ephemeral only in memory we risk losing it on a node failover." 
Author (Ava): "Good call — idempotency key is now stored in the session metadata (persisted to our session store with eventual expiration aligned to the quarantine TTL). Updated commit adds migration to ensure older sessions get a generated key on first touch."
- Lucas: "The soft-cancel branch looks reasonable, but we need a clear operator knob to lower the quarantine in high-load scenarios to avoid resource hoarding." 
Author: "Added server config 'stream.reconnect_quarantine_ms' and admit policy to the Dedicated config; also threw in metrics (stream.quarantine_claims, stream.quarantine_evictions) for SLO tracing."
- Zoe: "SDK behaviour: when a client receives a resume token and then immediately reconnects, do we double-ack function calls? We should document recommended client-side backoff." 
Author: "Added SDK helpers and docs recommending an exponential jittered reconnect up to the quarantine window; SDK also exposes 'resumeMode' with explicit 'resume' | 'restart' choices."

CI & testing summary: - CI pipeline initially failed one flaky e2e test (tests/e2e/stream_reconnect_spec.py::test_multiple_disconnects) on first run; root cause was non-deterministic test harness timing. Adjusted harness timing and reran. - All unit tests: pass - Integration: pass - E2E (re-run): pass - Benchmarks: p50 latency unchanged, p95 +3% under pathological reconnect storm (acceptable; documented and configuration knob added).

Migration & backward compatibility: The OpenAI adapter changes are behind a feature flag (compat.resume_v2). Existing sessions continue to use the legacy resume behavior until customers opt-in or until we flip the default after the deprecation window. SDKs will default to presenting the new behavior but will not force servers to adopt it.

Operational notes: Added two new metrics and one alert: stream.quarantine_claims (counter), stream.quarantine_evictions (counter), alert "HighStreamingQuarantineUtilization" (fires when >80% of streaming pool has claimed quarantine slots for >5min). Documentation includes mitigation (reduce quarantine_ms, increase pool capacity, or enable fast-hard-cancel).

Merge notes: squash-merged after approvals; no migration scripts required for customer data beyond session metadata backfill which is handled lazily on first resume touch.

If you want to exercise the new flow locally: see docs/api/streaming_resume.md and the e2e harness under tests/e2e/ for guidance on toggling the feature flag and simulating reconnects.

title: Introduce Toolcall Conductor with Async Signal Latching and OpenAI-compatible Result Hydration
preview: Context: Customers increasingly run multi-tool workflows where the model issues parallel function/tool calls and expects deterministic result composition, streaming stability, and graceful retries. Motivation: previous ad-hoc orchestration led to gibberish structured outputs, duplicate side-effects when retries happened, and brittle OpenAI-compat behavior for function-calling sequences. This PR introduces a new lightweight Toolcall Conductor that centralizes execution ergonomics: async signal latching to coordinate parallel tool runs, idempotent result hydration to prevent duplicate injections, and an OpenAI-compat shim for function-calling semantics in streamed responses. Summary of changes: 1) runtime: add redwood/pkg/runtime/toolcall/conductor.go implementing a conductor that tracks call IDs, latches result signals, and emits ordered hydration events to the streaming layer. 2) api: update redwood/pkg/api/openai_compat/handlers.go to translate OpenAI-style function_call choices into conductor jobs and to consume conductor hydration events for stable streamed structured output. 3) sdk: small ergonomics helpers in redwood-sdk-python/redwood/tooling.py and redwood-sdk-typescript/src/tooling.ts to expose a non-blocking call API that wires into the conductor. 4) client compat tests and e2e flows in benchmark-lab/test/toolcall_conductor_test.go and redwood-examples/multi-tool-orchestration/ to demonstrate retry-safe replay. 5) docs: new section in redwood-docs/reference/tool-calling.md describing the conductor lifecycle and recommended retry semantics. Key behavior & guarantees: - Async Signal Latching: parallel tool executions post a transient signal with a stable ID; the conductor latches the first successful result per signal ID and deduplicates subsequent completions. - Idempotent Result Hydration: hydration objects are stamped with stable request-call identifiers and replay-safe tokens so retries or replays will not cause duplicate injections or side-effects. - Streaming Stability: the OpenAI-compat shim buffers partial tool outputs and emits structured function_call + response blocks in the same ordering across initial stream and any reconnects. - Retry policy: default hedged-retry policy with configurable window; retries for non-idempotent tools are disabled by default and require opt-in via tool metadata. Migration notes: minor SDK change for tool registration signature (opt-in param "idempotent": true/false) and server-side default policy; existing tool integrations continue to work but will not get automatic retries until annotated. Backward compatibility: OpenAI-compat endpoints remain backwards-compatible for single-tool calls; multi-tool streaming semantics improved but explicit opt-in required in some edge cases. Checklist: - [x] Implement conductor core and unit tests - [x] Integrate with OpenAI-compat handler - [x] Add SDK ergonomics helpers and examples - [x] Add regression tests for retries and replay - [x] Update docs and migration guide Testing: unit tests for latch/dedup logic, integration tests covering streaming reconnection scenarios, manual QA on Dedicated and Hosted with synthetic multi-tool workloads, load tests in perf pipeline to validate latency impact. Risk & mitigations: potential perf overhead from conductor coordination under extreme fan-out; mitigated with batching windows and fast-path bypass for single-tool calls. Non-idempotent tools present risk of duplicate side-effects; default policy disables retries and docs + SDK require explicit idempotent flag. Related work: follow-ups expected to add observability hooks in Redwood Console for per-call idempotency rates and latency heatmaps. Commits summary: Commit a1b2c3 add conductor scaffolding and interface definitions; Commit d4e5f6 implement signal latch and dedupe; Commit f7g8h9 add openai_compat translations and streaming glue; Commit i0j1k2 SDK helpers and tests; Commit l3m4n5 docs and examples. Files changed (high level): ["redwood/pkg/runtime/toolcall/conductor.go","redwood/pkg/runtime/toolcall/conductor_test.go","redwood/pkg/api/openai_compat/handlers.go","redwood-sdk-python/redwood/tooling.py","redwood-sdk-typescript/src/tooling.ts","benchmark-lab/test/toolcall_conductor_test.go","redwood-examples/multi-tool-orchestration/demo.md","redwood-docs/reference/tool-calling.md","redwood-docs/migrations/2026-02-21-toolcall-conductor.md","redwood-console/observability/hooks_stub.go","redwood/ci/toolcall_conductor_e2e.yml","redwood/pkg/runtime/toolcall/retry_policy.go"].

title: prefill-granularity-adaptation-and-decode-slip-controller
preview: Context: Large variance in sequence-length mixes causes the static-chunking heuristic to either over-prefill (high tail latency) or under-utilize kernels (reduced throughput). This PR introduces two coordinated mechanisms: 1) a prefill granularity adapter that tunes chunk sizes per-worker based on online sequence-length statistics with hysteresis to avoid oscillation; 2) a decode-slip controller that detects and corrects 'decode slips' where decode work lags prefill progress, by selectively throttling new prefill chunks and prioritizing KV preheat for imminent decodes. Motivation: reduce p95/p99 tail latencies without sacrificing average throughput across mixed workloads. Summary of changes: - runtime/scheduler: add prefill_resizer module that maintains an EWMA of token lengths and chooses chunk targets; includes hysteresis thresholds and a bounded resize rate to keep stability; implements a small state machine to yield or coalesce chunk requests when downstream decode queue depth is high - runtime/scheduler: introduce decode_slip_controller that observes per-stream decode lag (tokens in prefill minus tokens decoded) and issues backpressure signals to the prefill allocator; exposes metrics for slip_score and corrective_actions - runtime/kernel: lightweight hint API to mark KV ranges for prioritized preheat so urgent decodes get hot KV - runtime/kvcache: add prioritized_get path used by decode_slip_controller to prefetch small warmups - perf tests and benchmarks: perf/prefill_adapt_bench.py and benchmark matrices in .github/workflows/perf-matrix.yml - docs: docs/architecture/prefill-adapt.md with operator knobs and recommended defaults Compatibility: behavior defaults conservatively to existing static chunking when feature gate is off. Rollout plan: start with Canary-1 region, enable on a 10% share for 48h, evaluate slip_score and p99 latency regressions. Checklist: - Design doc reviewed and linked (ENG-1948); - Unit tests for state machine and controller added; - Perf harness added and runs in CI; - Docs updated with operator guidance; - Feature gated and runtime flag controlled; - No public API changes; See also related PRs for scheduling heuristics and KV prefetching.

title: Sticky circuit rescue and tenant SLO lease reconciliation hotpatch
preview: Context: During a regional failover test we observed a class of "sticky" circuit states where a tenant's circuit stayed open on a subset of nodes because SLO lease renewal metadata was lost during transient control-plane reconvergence. This caused two poor outcomes: (1) recovery attempts kept evicting traffic from the recovering cohort and (2) telemetry showed mismatched SLO ownership leading to incorrect load-shedding decisions. Motivation: quick mitigation for production incidents plus a robust reconciliation path to prevent recurrence. This PR implements a two-part hotpatch: (A) Rescue path in the circuit manager that forces a short idempotent reconciliation handshake between the regional controller and node-local circuit store when a lease renewal is missing for >30s, and (B) SLO lease reconciliation routine that scans owner metadata and repairs mismatched leases with a conservative backoff and audit logging. Design details: - Added CircuitRescue handler in runtime/controller that detects stale circuit-owner gaps and attempts a best-effort rebind. The operation is idempotent and uses a compare-and-swap token to prevent races. - Introduced slo_lease_reconciler service (configurable via feature flag slo.lease_reconcile=true) that runs incremental passes and emits reconciliation metrics slo.reconciliations.total and slo.reconciliations.repaired. Pass logic favors non-disruptive repairs: if tenants show active traffic we issue a soft-binding and keep previous eviction thresholds. - Telemetry: emit audit logs for reconciliations (reconciler.audit) and an event when a circuit is forced-closed by rescue (circuit.rescue.forced_close). - Safety: rescue path uses a per-tenant rate limiter (max 1 repair per 2m) and will not perform eviction or token-bucket resets; it only rebinds owner metadata. - Rollout: feature-flagged and default-disabled. Hotpatch enabled for incident mitigation via feature flag in production for 3 days then evaluated. Backwards compatibility: operates against existing circuit-store schema, no migrations. Checklist: - [x] Rescue handler implemented in runtime/controller - [x] Reconciler service with metrics and audit logs - [x] Unit tests for reconciliation logic and compare-and-swap paths - [x] Integration test simulating lost lease across controller restart - [x] Feature flag and runtime config documented - [x] Observability dashboard queries added to SLO & Incident dashboards (one-liners) Files changed: runtime/circuit/manager.go, controller/slo/reconciler.go, telemetry/metrics_registry.yaml, integration-tests/reconcile_test.go, docs/operational/reconciliation.md Test plan: unit and integration tests included; staged rollout to canary region for 6 hours then global ramp if metrics stable. Related work: follows up on ENG-9182 and previously-reported incident SUP-1287.

title: Policy-driven gradient rollouts and shadow/A-B traffic orchestration
preview:
Motivation: provide a single policy layer that can express canary gradients, A/B splits, shadowing, and automated rollback triggers for model variant rollouts across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private deployments. This PR implements a policy-driven rollout engine and runtime hooks to route traffic incrementally, evaluate health metrics, and trigger automated rollback or promotion when thresholds are met.

What this change contains: checklist: [x] rollout policy DSL and parser; [x] runtime router integration (routing-by-policy); [x] metrics adapter to evaluate success criteria (latency, error-rate, quality-regression hooks); [x] shadow mode implementation (non-impacting mirrored requests); [x] A/B split support with weighted sampling; [x] automated rollback circuit with hysteresis and cooldown; [x] console integration for visualizing rollout state and editing policies; [x] e2e and unit tests with synthetic traffic scenarios.

Design notes: the policy DSL is deliberately conservative: it expresses a percentage gradient (start->target), evaluation windows, primary metrics with tolerated deltas, and fallback actions. Policies are evaluated by a lightweight controller: the controller emits routing directives to the router via an atomic config push (no hot-restart required). To avoid cross-tenant leakage the controller enforces model variant isolation and integrates with existing RBAC checks.

Rollout modes supported: 1) gradient canary (0->N% over time), 2) A/B experiments with deterministic assignment keys, 3) shadow/mirror traffic where responses are recorded and compared asynchronously, 4) manual promote/abort via console.

Safety: automated rollback is conservative — thresholds must be met for three consecutive windows and the controller requires a minimum sample size before taking action. A circuit-breaker prevents rapid flapping and records reasons in audit logs. Emergency abort can be triggered via API or console and will immediately revert routing directives.

Testing and validation: included e2e scenarios that run synthetic traffic mixes (latency skew, quality regressions simulated via metric stubs). We also added perf microbenchmarks ensuring router decision path adds <0.5ms p50 on our standard config.

Migration notes: existing rollouts configured with the older mirror/canary flags are automatically translated to the new policy model on startup with a one-time migration job. Admins can opt-out via the feature flag rollout.policy_migration=false.

Operational notes: rollout controller exposes metrics: rollout_progress, rollout_failure_count, rollback_count, policy_eval_latency_ms. Alerts recommended: rollout_failure_count > 0 for 5m and policy_eval_latency_ms > 250ms.

Related: ENG-7432 implements the policy DSL and engine; ENG-7500 wires the console UI and migration job.

title: introduce-session-lease-shim-cross-sdk-ergonomics-and-version-bumps
preview:
Motivation: We continue to see transient spikes in reauth calls and duplicated token refresh logic across SDKs, which increases tail latency for streaming workloads and complicates operator mental models. This PR introduces a cross-SDK 'session lease' shim: a short-lived leased token that the SDK refreshes proactively on a coordinated cadence, together with a small state machine that exposes lease health to the streaming layer. The goal is to reduce auth thrash, align retry/backoff semantics across languages, and provide ergonomic stream handles for application authors.

What this PR does (single-string summary): Adds SessionLeaseManager to Python, a Lease-aware Transport and StreamHandle to TypeScript, and a lease-enabled connection pool in Go. Harmonizes retry windows and backoff formulas for auth failures vs transient network errors. Adds ergonomic stream APIs: Python async iterator variant that yields typed frames, TypeScript streamHandle with close() and attemptResume(), and Go StreamSink with CancelableContext and LeaseInspection hooks. Also bumps SDK package versions and updates changelogs.

Design notes and constraints: Leases are short-lived (default 60s) and renewed proactively at 70% of lifetime. Renewals are serialized per process-connection (to avoid stampeding) and use randomized jitter. The shim is backward-compatible: if server does not advertise lease headers, the SDK falls back to existing token rotation behavior. No server contract change is required for forward compatibility, but servers can optionally send X-Session-Lease TTL headers to tune client behavior. We added telemetry hooks (lease.acquire/renew/fail) consumed by the console for debugging.

Commits (high-level):
- d4f1a2b: introduce SessionLeaseManager interface and local tests (python)
- e6b3c9f: add lease-aware transport adapter and stream handle to ts-sdk
- a7c21d4: integrate lease manager with go-transport and clientpool
- c8f9b11: harmonize retry/backoff constants and unit tests across SDKs
- b5a2f77: ergonomics: python aiter_stream_frames, ts streamHandle.close, go StreamSink.Cancel
- f2d0a3e: bump versions, changelogs, and release notes

Changed areas (representative):
- redwood-sdk-python/redwood/session_lease.py (new)
- redwood-sdk-python/redwood/streaming/async_iter.py (modified)
- redwood-sdk-python/setup.cfg, pyproject.toml (version bump)
- redwood-sdk-typescript/src/lease/leaseManager.ts (new)
- redwood-sdk-typescript/src/stream/streamHandle.ts (modified)
- redwood-sdk-typescript/package.json (version bump)
- redwood-sdk-go/lease/manager.go (new)
- redwood-sdk-go/transport/pool.go (modified)
- docs/sdk/session-lease.md (new)
- docs/sdk/upgrade-guides/1.17_to_1.18.md (modified)

Small illustrative snippet (ts streamHandle change):
- before: export class StreamHandle { close(): Promise<void> { /* ... */ } }
- after: export class StreamHandle { async close(graceful: boolean = true): Promise<void> { /* honor lease-aware drain */ } async attemptResume(): Promise<boolean> { /* try resume using cached cursor */ } }

Review and CI summary: First CI run failed on TS lint (prettier) and one flaky integration test in Go clientpool under heavy load. The failures were addressed in follow-up commits: fixed formatting, added racy test guard, and tuned a backoff edge-case that surfaced under the integration harness. Final CI: all checks green (unit, lint, integration on linux/amd64 and macos/x86_64). Full CI details available in the checks tab.

title: Introduce lightweight lint runtime pool and dependency grooming task
preview:
Problem: presubmit lint jobs are expensive and noisy; transient dependency spikes increase CI latency. This PR introduces a lightweight, long-lived lint runtime pool (shared JVM/Node environments) plus a scheduled dependency grooming task to keep devdeps compact. Goals: reduce presubmit latency variance, cut install IO, and make small dependency churn visible for engineering teams.

What this PR does (summary):
- Adds a new presubmit runner material: .github/workflows/presubmit-lightweight.yml that routes lint-only PRs into the lightweight pool.
- Implements a lint pool coordinator: ci/lint/pool_coordinator.go (small state machine, in-memory leases with optional Redis backing).
- Replaces heavy per-job toolchain bootstrap for JS/TS linters with a cached shared environment (ci/images/presubmit-lint.tar).
- Adds tools/dep-groom.sh, a scheduled task (GH Action scheduled workflow) that runs `npm audit --json`-style checks, reports candidate devdep removals in an issue, and pins transient transitive dependencies when safe.
- Adds metrics and tracing hooks: observability/lint_pool.md and light opentelemetry spans in the coordinator.

Why now: recent infra telemetry showed a 30% tail latency increase in presubmit stage during peak weekdays (Q1), driven by fluctuating package install times and cold runner boot. The lightweight pool removes repeated full bootstrap costs and surfaces dependency churn for owners to handle proactively.

Backward compatibility and rollout: default main presubmit lane unchanged. New lightweight lane is opt-in by adding label `ci:light-lint` (and later we will add detection for lint-only changes). The dep-groom workflow runs weekly and creates issues rather than auto-mutating lockfiles for safety.

Checklist:
- [x] Add lightweight GH workflow and pool coordinator implementation
- [x] Add small shim script to create/restore cached toolchain images
- [x] Add scheduled dep-groom workflow and reporting stub
- [x] Add observability docs and dashboard stubs
- [x] Unit tests for coordinator lease behavior
- [x] Integration smoke run using CI preview environment

Commits summary: 
- 7 commits: initial coordinator prototype; workflow and image shim; dep-groom script + report template; unit tests; docs and dashboard; CI fixes; squash and tidy.

High-level files changed (examples): 
- .github/workflows/presubmit-lightweight.yml
- .github/workflows/dep-groom-schedule.yml
- ci/lint/pool_coordinator.go
- ci/images/Makefile (add presubmit-lint image target)
- ci/scripts/cache_toolchain.sh
- tools/dep-groom.sh
- observability/lint_pool.md
- tests/ci/lint_pool_test.go

CI notes: initial run failed on flaky image extraction (fixed in commit 4). Unit tests pass locally and in CI after the fix. Nightly smoke run against the preview cluster validated end-to-end path (job leased, runner executed, artifacts uploaded).

Rollout plan (post-merge): enable lightweight lane for the infra and docs teams for 1 week, measure presubmit median and p95 latency, then expand opt-in labels to auto-detect lint-only PRs. Dep-groom will be observed for 4 cycles before any automation for lockfile edits is considered.

Related Linear tickets: ENG-4021 (presubmit latency investigation), ENG-4090 (dep grooming spec). Related past work: some ideas derived from presubmit-minifier RFC and prior presubmit-prober experiments.

Notes for reviewers: focus review on coordinator lease edge-cases, security around cached images (we do not elevate privileges), and the dep-groom report format (it intentionally avoids automated changes).

title: Introduce slim presubmit quickcheck and dependency entropy mitigator
preview:
Context: We are observing long presubmit runtime spikes and noisy dependency churn which cause slower feedback and occasional release regressions. This PR introduces two coordinated interventions: 1) a 'quickcheck' presubmit lane that runs a tiny, fast smoke subset of lint+unit checks for rapid developer feedback, dynamically escalating to full presubmit on larger diffs; 2) a Dependency Entropy Mitigator (DE-Mitigator), a policy + CI job that detects high-velocity transitive changes and applies conservative auto-fix suggestions (lockfile pinning, devdep pruning), plus a dry-run bot that submits candidate fixes for approval.

What I changed (high level): Consolidated presubmit orchestration into an explicit stage graph (presubmit.quickcheck -> presubmit.full -> presubmit.post-merge), added a tiny Node/Go lint shard runner and a lightweight python test-sandbox for fast isolation, implemented the DE-Mitigator analyzer (go-based), introduced new CI annotations for upward escalation thresholds, and updated release hooks to tolerate transient dep-refresh windows.

Why: Reduce median feedback time from ~7m to ~90s for small changes; reduce noisy human triage from rolling dependency churn; prevent release automation failures caused by simultaneous transitive updates.

Checklist:
- [x] New quickcheck lane + docs
- [x] DE-Mitigator prototype and CI integration (dry-run mode)
- [x] Backwards-compatible release hook changes and graceful fallback
- [x] Unit + smoke integration tests
- [x] Rollout plan: opt-in for 1 week then default-on for teams with >50% presubmit hits

text:
Context: We are observing long presubmit runtime spikes and noisy dependency churn which cause slower feedback and occasional release regressions. This PR introduces two coordinated interventions: 1) a 'quickcheck' presubmit lane that runs a tiny, fast smoke subset of lint+unit checks for rapid developer feedback, dynamically escalating to full presubmit on larger diffs; 2) a Dependency Entropy Mitigator (DE-Mitigator), a policy + CI job that detects high-velocity transitive changes and applies conservative auto-fix suggestions (lockfile pinning, devdep pruning), plus a dry-run bot that submits candidate fixes for approval.

What I changed (high level): Consolidated presubmit orchestration into an explicit stage graph (presubmit.quickcheck -> presubmit.full -> presubmit.post-merge), added a tiny Node/Go lint shard runner and a lightweight python test-sandbox for fast isolation, implemented the DE-Mitigator analyzer (go-based), introduced new CI annotations for upward escalation thresholds, and updated release hooks to tolerate transient dep-refresh windows.

Why: Reduce median feedback time from ~7m to ~90s for small changes; reduce noisy human triage from rolling dependency churn; prevent release automation failures caused by simultaneous transitive updates.

title: Multiplexer: parallel tool execution, idempotent retries, and ergonomic result injection
preview:
Motivation: production workloads increasingly issue multiple tool calls per response (lookups, enrichment, external actions) and clients expect predictable injection semantics, low-latency parallelism, and transparent retry behavior when external tools fail. This PR introduces a lightweight multiplexer for tool execution that: 1) executes independent tool calls in parallel when safe, 2) exposes an explicit idempotency token + retry middleware for transient tool failures, 3) provides an ergonomic result-injection API surface for handlers (deterministic injection points with ordering hints), and 4) keeps OpenAI-compatibility behaviors (function-calling fallback and structured outputs) intact.

Summary of changes: commits implement a runtime-level ToolMultiplexer that accepts a list of ToolCall requests and returns deterministic ToolResults with stable ids and insertion points. We added a RetryMiddleware that respects per-tool idempotency tokens and exponential backoff policies, and a backpressure-aware worker pool that bounds concurrency based on route config. The compatibility shim for openai->redwood function calling was updated to translate function return values into the new ToolResult shape. Documentation and SDK ergonomics were updated (Python and TS examples) and end-to-end tests were added for mixed success/failure scenarios.

title: soft-slot-compaction-and-reverse-probe-continuous-batcher-tuning
preview: Motivation: tail latency and occasional starvation remain a concern for long-lived continuous batching workloads that mix very small and very large KV-aware requests. Existing admission logic can leave low-priority small requests waiting behind large KV-heavy slots for extended periods, especially under transient load spikes and kernel-switch latencies. This PR introduces two coordinated mechanisms: 1) Soft slot compaction: a background compaction pass that opportunistically collapses underutilized slots to reduce fragmentation and improve batch packing without violating latency SLOs; 2) Reverse probe (starvation recovery probe): a low-overhead probe that periodically injects high-priority short-circuit admissions from the tail of the queue to validate progress and trigger rebalancing when starvation is detected. Design summary: soft slot compaction tracks slot utilization windows and merges adjacent underutilized soft slots when the expected merge latency benefit outweighs requeue cost; compaction runs with adaptive backoff tuned by observed kernel context-switch cost and current throughput. Reverse probe uses a lightweight preempt token that can be granted to small requests older than a configurable threshold; if a probe succeeds it increments a decay counter and relaxes admission gating for similar requests for a short epoch. Both features are KV-aware: compaction respects KV pin locality hints and avoids merging slots across incompatible KV caches; probes include a kernel hint so the runtime can choose a faster, lower-quantization kernel path when available. Implementation notes: scheduler.go implements soft slot compaction state machine and compaction heuristics; kv_cache.go exposes lease-safe merge primitives; selection.go includes a fast-path for probe-elected admissions and kernel_hint metadata; a new metrics set is added (batcher.compaction.* and batcher.probe.*) and knobs exposed via the dynamic config system. Testing and benchmarks: microbenchmarks with mixed 80/20 small/large request distributions show 95th tail latency improvement of 22-28% in steady-state and 30-45% improvement under spike scenarios with preserved median latencies. Throughput impact is +/- 2% depending on the workload; CPU overhead for compaction is measured at <0.6% on our benchmark fleet. Rollout plan: feature toggles default off in regions with strict latency SLOs; gradual rollout using canary groups and telemetry gating. Fallback: compaction and reverse probe are reversible via config, and compaction only performs non-destructive merges; if regressions are observed the rollout will be halted and automatic rollback triggered. Related work: builds on earlier admission and holdback experiments (see ENG-1122, ENG-1209), and complements prefix/KV caching improvements in ENG-1256. Documentation: RFC added under docs/rfc/soft_slot_compaction.md and runtime knobs documented in ops/console. Risks and mitigations: compaction could increase requeue churn if misconfigured; mitigated by conservative defaults, adaptive backoff, and safety guards that prevent compaction when kernel latencies exceed configured thresholds. The PR includes extensive unit and integration tests and a release note entry.
