title: v1/chat.completions: resilient stream-fragmenting + truncated-logprobs + toolcall backoff
preview:
Motivation: We've observed two classes of production failures in the OpenAI /v1/chat.completions compatibility adapter when tool/function calling and long-running model responses are involved: 1) clients receiving partial or duplicated streamed chunks after transient network blips or internal retries, and 2) malformed structured outputs (JSON) emitted incrementally by the model which break downstream tool parsing. Additionally, customers running toolcall heavy workloads saw elevated tail error rates when the function invocation subsystem experienced transient 5xxs or long latencies.

This PR implements a set of protocol and runtime changes to improve UX and parity with typical OpenAI semantics while keeping streaming stable:
- Stream fragment reconciliation: attach a "stream_fragment_id" UUID to every top-level event in the stream envelope and an optional "resume_token" when resuming. The client-side SDKs can re-assemble idempotent fragments and drop duplicates after reconnect. This reduces double-insert issues observed in clients that reconnect mid-response.
- Truncated logprobs envelope: instead of omitting logprobs for long token runs, we add a "logprobs_truncated": true flag and a "last_token_index" field so clients can reconcile partial probability data without mis-attributing tokens. This preserves parity with expected OpenAI logprobs semantics for downstream evaluators.
- Toolcall backoff and fallback policy: the toolcall manager now implements exponential backoff with jitter for transient tool failures (5xx/timeout). After 3 retries or 10s cumulative delay we emit a structured fallback event and mark the function call as "failed_retryable" in the response envelope. Admin-configurable policy allows immediate fallback to a non-tool resolution path (e.g., model-only response) for customers that prefer availability over tool fidelity.
- Structured output sanitizer: streaming JSON fragments are validated by a permissive streaming parser. We now buffer up to 4KB of partial JSON fragments per toolcall and attempt best-effort correction for common issues (trailing commas, unclosed braces) before sending final structured outputs. If sanitizer cannot repair, we emit a "structured_output_error" event with the raw fragment for downstream diagnostics.
- Client heartbeat: server emits a small "heartbeat" control event every 18s on long streams to keep HTTP/HTTP2 intermediaries alive and to surface stuck connections earlier to clients.

Changes included (summary):
- API/compat: redwood/api/compat/chatv1_adapter.py -- add fragment_id/resume_token header handling, map truncated logprobs into new envelope keys, and wire toolcall fallback flags.
- Runtime: redwood/runtime/streaming/fragment_coordinator.go -- fragment generation and dedupe table + resume token handling.
- Tooling: redwood/runtime/tooling/toolcall_manager.go -- retry/backoff policy, fallback event emission, metrics counters.
- Observability: redwood/observability/metrics/streaming_metrics.promcfg -- new metrics: stream.fragment.reconciled, toolcall.retry.count, structured_output.errors.
- SDKs: redwood-sdk-python/streaming_client.py -- resume token logic + heartbeat handling; redwood-sdk-typescript/streaming.ts -- same. Tests added to both SDKs.
- Tests: unit tests for fragment coordinator, integration test that simulates mid-stream disconnect and verifies idempotent reconstruction, e2e canary with perf-canary harness.

Checklist:
- [x] Protocol fields and server wiring
- [x] Backoff and fallback policy implementation
- [x] Streaming sanitizer + error event path
- [x] SDK resume/heartbeat logic (Python & TS)
- [x] Unit/integration/e2e tests
- [x] Benchmarks: perf-canary workload run and dashboard updated

Performance notes:
- Microbenchmark: median latency unchanged; 95th percentile latency increased ~1.8% at 2048 token workloads due to additional fragment metadata and sanitizer buffering.
- Error-rate experiments: toolcall transient failure recovery reduced end-user visible function-failure errors from 6.4% -> 1.2% in staging under injected 5xxs.

Operational notes:
- New metrics are in the streaming metrics group and exported to the dashboard. Alerting: added a throttled alert on structured_output.errors to detect unexpected sanitizer regressions.
- Defaults: backoff policy defaults are conservative (max_retries=3, base_delay=250ms, max_delay=5s) and can be overridden via tenant config in Dedicated/Private installs.

Related work and references: Linear ENG-9786 (stream resilience), ticket SUP-4321 (customer report), related PRs for chat-stream framing in history.

Merging: squash merge into main after green CI and a short canary in prod (rolled to 10% traffic) succeeded.

title: dynamic per-channel requant + KV-aware jitter-resilient continuous-batching
preview: Background and motivation: Production workloads show variance in per-channel activation distributions across prompts and token positions which makes static requant scales suboptimal. We observed systematic p99 latency spikes when the runtime switched between hot and cold KV cache footprints under continuous-batching; these were exacerbated by small batching jitter when multiple streams aligned. This PR implements a dynamic per-channel requant stage combined with a KV-cache-aware scheduling hint and a jitter-resilient continuous-batching policy to smooth throughput while keeping low tail latency. Scope: 1) A lightweight per-channel scale estimator that runs on the microkernel hot path and updates requant multipliers at configurable amortization windows. 2) A cheap, lock-free KV cache sizing hint that the scheduler uses to avoid abrupt prefix evictions during batch coalescing. 3) A jitter-resilient continuous-batcher that introduces minimal, adaptive hold windows to align requests from high-variance sources without increasing median latency. 4) Microkernel changes to expose a fused transform that applies requant multipliers inline with dequant/attn input packing to eliminate an extra copy. Design highlights and tradeoffs: - Estimator runs every N tokens (configurable per model) and uses exponential moving moments to remain stable across bursts. The goal is to reduce per-token compute overhead to <0.5% while adapting scales within 5-10k tokens. - Scheduler hint is advisory: it tags requests with estimated KV working set delta. The placement layer still enforces hard SLOs; the hint avoids placing requests that would cause a working-set jump on the same GPU within a short window. - Batcher employs an adaptive jitter budget: it only delays the head of a queue by up to X ms if the expected throughput improvement outweighs the added latency; X decays if p99 latency rises. Benchmarks (representative): - OPT-13B, INT8 hybrid (smoothquant + per-channel dynamic re-quant): throughput +10.8% on steady-state mixed sized prompts, median latency +2% (due to micro-batching alignment), p99 latency -14% on tail-heavy traces. - Llama-2-7B FP8 fallback: end-to-end token cost reduced by ~7% due to fewer KV evictions and reduced memory copies. - End-to-end e2e test with 500 simulated concurrent streams: observed 8-12% lower GPU-side memcpy time and 6-9% better sustained throughput. Implementation notes: - Runtime changes: src/runtime/quant/dyn_requant.h/cpp, src/runtime/quant/microkernel_requant.s, src/runtime/scheduler/kv_hint_scheduler.cpp - Batcher: src/runtime/batching/jitter_batcher.cpp, with a new config block in configs/batching.yaml - Tests: tests/quant/test_dyn_requant.cc (unit), bench/latency/bench_kv_jitter.py (integration traces) - Docs: docs/perf/quantization.md updated with configuration knobs and suggested Linear tasks for tuning. Checklist: - [x] Unit tests for estimator accuracy and stability - [x] Integration benchmark harness with trace replay - [x] Backwards-compatibility: dynamic requant disabled by default; opt-in via runtime config - [x] Release notes draft included - [x] SRE smoke test run on dedicated cluster and passed Notes on safety and compatibility: This change touches kernel hot-paths; dynamic scaling is guarded behind a runtime flag and tuned conservatively out-of-the-box to avoid regression. We included an automatic rollback condition in the scheduler when p99 exceeds baseline by 20% during an experiment. Related work: ENG-2048 (per-channel requant research spike), PR #612345 (microkernel packing refactor) for reference. Next steps: 1) A follow-up PR to expose an admin dashboard for per-model scale drift metrics and recommended amortization windows. 2) Experiment with a learned estimator offline and compare to the EMA-based estimator used here.

title: hybrid flashattn + lazy-kv-eviction + operator-fusion for long-seq workloads
preview:
Motivation: Production workloads with mixed short- and long-context requests show uneven tail latency and high aggregate token cost due to conservative KV cache retention and generic attention kernel selection. This PR introduces a hybrid attention path that (1) dispatches FlashAttention when safe, (2) uses a lightweight fused matmul-add-dropout kernel for mid-sized heads, and (3) falls back to the existing fallback kernel for pathological shapes. In parallel, we implement a lazy KV eviction policy that decouples KV entry lifetime from request completion and enables opportunistic eviction under memory pressure. Together these changes reduce long-sequence p99 latency and improve throughput for streaming workloads while keeping memory bounded.

What changed (high level): implemented a Flash-aware scheduler that picks kernel variant at invocation time based on sequence length, head size, and kv-cache residency; added a fused QKV->matmul+add kernel for medium shapes (mp size 16/32) to reduce kernel launch overhead; integrated a lazy-kv-eviction manager that uses LRU+age heuristics and signals the scheduler to prefer compact kernels when eviction is active; CI/build: updated CUDA kernels, operator registration, and added unit/integration tests; docs: runtime tuning doc and rollout checklist.

Benchmarks (representative): evaluated on 8xA100-80GB and local dev GPU (A5000). Long-seq streaming (seq=16384, batch=1) shows p50 latency -12%, p95 -23%, p99 -31%, tokens/sec +18% vs main with previous FlashAttention dispatch. Medium mixed workload (seq distribution 128-2048) throughput improved +12% and reduced kernel launch counts by ~28%. Memory: average KV memory footprint reduced ~9% due to lazy eviction behavior under synthetic tail traffic.

Testing & CI: unit tests for kernel selection logic, KV eviction invariants, and end-to-end streaming harness. Added synthetic replay tests for real production traces. CI initially failed on quantization edge-case in fused kernel (nvcc error for sm_80 path), fixed by tightening alignments and adding memcpy fallback for odd strides. Full CI now green.

Rollout plan: feature-gated behind runtime.experimental.hybrid_attention. Staged rollout: Canary on Dedicated pool -> 10% Hosted -> 50% -> full. Canary will enable additional tracing hooks for per-request kernel decisions and kv-eviction events. Rollback path: flip feature flag or restart runtime to main kernel selection. The PR does not change public API, no breaking change.

Checklist: - [x] Kernel selection heuristics implemented and unit-tested - [x] Fused kernel validated on sm_70/75/80 - [x] Lazy KV eviction has safety invariants and metrics - [x] End-to-end perf harness and regression alerts - [x] Rollout feature flag and tracing - [x] Docs & tuning guide updated

title: Adaptive jittered flush and downstream-aware credit pacing for smoother streaming
preview: Summary: Implements three coordinated runtime changes to reduce streaming tail and burst latency for token-generation workloads: (1) a jittered flush window that spreads microbatch flushes to avoid aligned burst tails; (2) a downstream-latency-aware credit allocator that paces token credits based on observed consumer RTT and in-flight decode headroom; (3) a speculative KV prefix hint prefetch that warms per-GPU KV cache for likely next-token prefixes using a lightweight in-memory prefix index. Goal: smooth token emission, reduce 95/99p TTFB for streaming clients, and avoid unnecessary kernel wakeups while keeping median latency neutral. Motivation and context: Production canaries and incidents (ENG-4823, ENG-4899) showed recurring tail spikes when many concurrent streams synchronized flushes at token boundaries or periodic intervals. Prior PRs mitigated parts of this, but correlation remained across streams, producing CPU/GPU contention and simultaneous KV cache churn. This change targets coordination between emitter, scheduler, and KV hinting to reduce correlated bursts without increasing steady-state cost. Design: JitteredFlushWindow: each emitter derives a small per-request jitter from request id + start time; flushes are randomized inside a window with an adaptive slack that shrinks when congestion is low. RTT-aware CreditPacer: credits are paced using a moving estimate of downstream RTT (transport/ACK hooks). Allocator reduces burst credits when downstream latency rises and opportunistically issues larger credits when downstream is responsive and batch headroom exists. KV Speculative Hints: maintain a light-weight probabilistic prefix index populated from recent prompts and frequent token transitions; when high-confidence candidates exist, schedule a low-cost prefetch of relevant KV pages into the local per-GPU cache before the microbatch reaches its max tokens. Prefetching uses a shared budget and is disabled under high cache pressure. Implementation notes and safety knobs: jitter default 20ms ±10ms; adaptive slack 0..50ms controlled by --emit.jitter-slack-ms. CreditPacer exposes --emit.credit-min, --emit.credit-max, --emit.credit-rtt-scale; pacing never increases credits above configured max. KV prefetch is feature-flagged and per-GPU budgeted; disabled on Dedicated/Private by default. Observability: per-request jitter offset, credit decisions, prefetch hits/misses, and cache pressure metrics emitted to metrics pipeline and console. Benchmarks (canary): aggregate micro-canary across 8 r5g instances plus GPU pool with 5k steady concurrent streams showed: 95p TTFB improved ~18% (220ms -> 180ms), 99p improved ~26% (410ms -> 305ms), median ~+2% (neutral). Kernel wakeups at flush boundaries down ~33%. KV cache cold-miss rate for warmed prefixes down ~45%. Rollout plan: Step 1: canary enable JitteredFlushWindow + CreditPacer; KV prefetch disabled. Step 2: enable KV prefetch in canary with low cache pressure and monitor two weeks. Step 3: staged rollout to host pools with metrics-driven gating (require 95p improvement and no error rate increases). Emergency revert: runtime flag --emit.disable-global disables the new behavior instantly. Tests and checklist: unit tests for jitter and credit pacing; integration tests with synthetic slow readers; load canary results uploaded to Linear ENG-4823; metrics and debug traces added; feature flags and knobs available for staged rollout. Commits summary (high level): feat(emitter): add JitteredFlushWindow and config knobs; feat(scheduler): plumb RTT signals to credit allocator and implement CreditPacer; feat(kv): add PrefixHintIndex and speculative prefetch path; test: unit + integration tests; ci: canary pipeline job for end-to-end streaming validation. Changed areas (high level): runtime/emit/emitter.go, runtime/scheduler/credit_pacer.go, runtime/kv/prefix_hint_index.go, runtime/kv/prefetch_manager.go, api/gateway/transport_hooks.go, observability/metrics/emit_metrics.go, test/integration/stream_slow_reader_test.go. Small illustrative snippet (emitter jitter calc): old: flushAt = lastEmit + flushInterval ; new: jitter = (hash(req.id) % jitterWindow) - jitterWindow/2 ; flushAt = lastEmit + flushInterval + jitter + adaptiveSlack. Review summary: reviewers raised concerns about rate-limit interactions and Dedicated memory pressure; addressed by ensuring jitter never reduces credits below configured minima, adding tests for rate-limit invariants, disabling KV prefetch on Dedicated/Private by default, adding per-GPU budgeting and LRU eviction cap. Metric naming nit (emit.jitter_offset_ms -> emit.jitter_offset_millis) fixed. CI summary: unit: pass; integration: pass; lint: pass; canary-sim: pass; total: pass. Merge: squash-merge to main by Mateo Ruiz referencing ENG-4823 and ENG-4899. Release notes: Adaptive jittered flush and downstream-aware credit pacing reduce streaming tail latency (95/99p); optional KV speculative prefetch warms high-confidence prefixes; opt-in via runtime flags; staged rollout performed. Notes: favors smoothing over aggressive micro-optimizations; follow-ups planned to combine with kernel coalescing heuristics and workload-aware batching.

title: Improve chat stream framing, function-call handshake, and logprob envelope
preview:
Motivation: We observed intermittent corruption of streamed messages when function calls were emitted mid-chunk and when stop sequences split across buffer boundaries. Consumers (third-party SDKs and internal examples) reported mis-attributed logprobs and truncated function-call payloads. This causes downstream tooling that relies on deterministic final outputs and token-level diagnostics to misbehave.

Changes in this PR: added a lightweight framing state machine to the streaming runtime so every SSE/data chunk is annotated with a stable boundary token and an explicit event for function-call lifecycle: 'fn_call.start' and 'fn_call.end'. This removes ambiguity about when a tool invocation begins and ends across stream re-fragments. To surface token-level diagnostics without polluting the incremental stream we emit a final 'logprob_envelope' JSON object as the last event for both streaming and non-streaming requests; it maps token offsets to per-role logprobs and is documented as a separate (optional) payload consumers can request.

Backward compatibility: The new framing is opt-out for existing clients by default. We add a query parameter include_logprob_envelope (true|false) and a legacy alias logprobs that maps to the same behavior for OpenAI-style callers. Existing simple callers that don't parse the terminal envelope see no change. The handshake events are idempotent and can be ignored by clients that only read incremental content.

Behavioral guarantees: stop sequence handling has been unified: when a stop token spans a chunk boundary the runtime defers finalization until the full stop is observed and then emits the terminal event in the same atomic sequence across streaming and non-streaming modes. Function-call arguments are emitted as a single atomic object once the 'fn_call.end' event occurs. The logprob envelope is computed using the final KV-cache state to ensure token logprobs are consistent with the produced text.

Performance and safety: microbenchmarks show a small median latency regression (~2%) for small requests due to additional framing metadata, but a ~12% reduction in upstream token compute in workloads that reuse prefixes because of improved KV-cache reuse in the stream path. All code paths guard against exposing private keys or internal tracing identifiers in the emitted envelope.

Tests and verification: added unit tests for framing transitions, integration tests that simulate chunked TCP delivery and function-call mid-stream, and e2e tests verifying parity between streaming and blocking endpoints on stop/logprob outputs. CI initially failed the integration matrix due to a flaky test that assumed synchronous framing; that test was updated and the CI rerun passed.

Docs and examples: added a short migration note and an examples recipe showing how SDKs should collect 'fn_call.start'/'fn_call.end' events and parse the final 'logprob_envelope' for diagnostics.

Notes for reviewers: major areas to focus on are the correctness of framing transitions under network re-fragmentation, the atomicity of function-call object emission, and the memory footprint of the logprob envelope on very long responses. The PR is intentionally conservative in toggles to allow gradual rollout and feature-flagged experiments.

title: concurrent-tool-runner: parallel tool invocation + OpenAI function_call compatibility updates
preview: Motivation: customers increasingly chain multiple tool calls per turn and want lower tail latency by running compatible tools in parallel, plus predictable result injection and idempotent retries. This PR introduces a server-side concurrent tool runner, extends the OpenAI-compatible function_call surface to allow explicit parallel calls and tool-result references, and updates SDK helpers and streaming merge semantics to keep the API stable for existing clients. Summary of changes: 1) runtime: add services/inference/tool_runner/concurrent_runner with a pluggable execution strategy (sequential | parallel | hybrid), a run_id-based idempotency layer for retries, and deterministic merge ordering for structured output injection. 2) api/openai-compat: extend function_call payload with optional keys: "parallel_calls" (array of tool names), "tool_result_refs" (map-like list of result identifiers), and "injection_policy" (deterministic | merge_stream | attach_raw). The compat layer translates these into internal execution plans and exposes merged streaming boundaries to callers. 3) streaming: improve chunk merging to reduce interleaving artifacts when multiple tool streams are active; add a small backpressure window to avoid head-of-line blocking in multi-tool flows. 4) SDKs: python & typescript helpers to create and await parallel tool futures, helpers to submit a single composite call that the server will execute in parallel when possible, and a deterministic merge utility for client-side local execution. 5) docs & tests: update openapi spec, add e2e test harness that exercises parallel function calls, and add unit tests covering retry idempotency and injection ordering. Backwards compatibility: default behavior remains sequential tool execution; the new keys are opt-in. Also preserved the original function_call semantics for single-tool usage to avoid breaking existing OpenAI-compatible clients. Rationale: moving parallelism into the platform lets us optimize batching and GPU utilization while keeping SDK ergonomics simple; adding run_id/idempotency reduces duplicate side-effects for retried calls. Rollout plan: feature gated behind runtime flag concurrent_tool_runner.enabled. Initially enabled in staging and can be opt-in per-tenant via config; will be enabled by default after 2 weeks of telemetry. Related: linked_linear ENG-4123 ENG-4181. Notes: this PR intentionally avoids introducing a new RPC surface; changes are translations within the compat layer and runtime to minimize client churn.

title: introduce-warmth-oracle-and-burst-fidelity-adapter
preview: Context and motivation: large tenants and bursty workloads create a repeated operational pattern: 1) cold clusters take time to reach steady KV-cache and batching efficiency, 2) ad-hoc bursts either exhaust soft quotas or produce high-per-tenant latency spikes, and 3) telemetry shows large oscillations in utilization that make predictive autoscaling noisy. This PR introduces two cooperating components — the Warmth Oracle and the Burst Fidelity Adapter — to reduce cold-start cost, stabilize autoscaling signals, and enforce tenant-level burst fidelity guarantees without hard-cap preemption. Goals: reduce cold-start tail latency for bursty tenants, smooth utilization signals used by the autoscaler, and provide per-tenant fidelity telemetry so SREs/product teams can reason about burst degradation. What this implements: - Warmth Oracle: lightweight estimator that maintains a time-decayed warmth score per cluster-pool and per-tenant fingerprint; uses recent warm-pool hit rate, KV-cache reuse ratio, and sequence-length histograms to suggest prewarm budgets and warmset priorities. It exposes two gRPC endpoints consumed by the fleet-provisioner and by the autoscaler heuristic. - Burst Fidelity Adapter (BFA): a soft enforcement layer between admission and the tenancy ledger that maps incoming burst requests to a fidelity budget. BFA prefers graceful fidelity reduction (e.g., reduce concurrency slice, slight quantization fallback, incremental batch size reduction) over outright rejections and emits fidelity-decisions as telemetry. - Telemetry and reporting: new metrics (warmth_score, fidelity_decision, fidelity_loss_estimate, tenant_fidelity_p50/p90) added to the observability-pack; a new /internal/api/v1/tenant_fidelity_report endpoint for on-demand sampling. - Autoscaler integration: the cluster autoscaler now consumes warmth_score to stabilize scale-up thresholds and to weight predicted headroom. This reduces scale oscillation for pools serving many cold-starting tenants. - Safety/fallbacks: if Warmth Oracle is unavailable, the autoscaler reverts to the previous warmset heuristic; BFA always falls back to a conservative reject path and records incidents for review. Implementation notes: - Majority of logic is in runtime/orchestration/warmth_oracle and runtime/autoscaler/adapters/burst_fidelity_adapter. - Warmth scoring is intentionally conservative: scores range 0..100 and update with exponential smoothing (alpha=0.08 default). - BFA decisions are config-driven per-tenant tier (free/standard/premium) with configurable fidelity ceilings. - Added an integration test harness that simulates multi-tenant burst patterns and validates that fidelity degradation follows expected policy. Testing and results: - Microbenchmarks: negligible CPU overhead (<= 1% median) when enabled on a standard worker node. - Integration test: simulated 100 tenant bursts; tail p95 cold-start latency improved by ~22% for affected tenants and autoscaler scale-up events reduced by ~18% in the synthetic workload. - Observability: new counters allow post-hoc attribution of fidelity loss to policy vs capacity constraints. Risks and mitigations: - Risk: tuning warmth_alpha incorrectly could under- or over-prioritize prewarming. Mitigation: default alpha conservative and surfaced in console for runtime tuning. - Risk: fidelity reductions might create user-visible regressions. Mitigation: tier-aware ceilings and runtime opt-out flags for premium tenants. Rollout plan: 1) Canary in a single non-prod region for 72h (feature flag off by default). 2) Gradual ramp to 10% of production pools, monitor fidelity_loss_estimate and p95. 3) Full enable with monitoring playbooks and rollback gates. Related: references ENG-1428 (predictive warm-pool advisor) and SUP-8812 (support ticket re: burst failures).

title: reservation-triggered-throttle-and-utilization-wavelet-reporter
preview: Context: Dedicated fleets sometimes oscillate between being underutilized and briefly saturated during short bursts. Existing burst controls rely on global accounting windows which either respond too late or over-aggressively for reservation-backed traffic. This PR introduces a reservation-triggered throttle that activates when a reservation's consumption pattern crosses configurable safety thresholds, and a complementary utilization wavelet reporter that provides smoothed, multi-scale utilization signals for autoscalers and SLO monitors. Motivation: reduce noisy evictions and over-provisioning, improve SLO preservation for reservation-backed tenants, and provide more actionable utilization telemetry for downstream controllers. What this changes: 1) Runtime: add a reservation-level throttle controller that monitors per-reservation token/slot consumption and emits soft-throttle decisions when short-timescale spike characteristics exceed the reservation's burst envelope. The throttle is implemented as a cooperative gate (non-destructive) that reduces accepted concurrency and gates new burst credits for the reservation while preserving headroom for latency-sensitive requests. 2) Telemetry: introduce the "util.wavelet" metric family which reports wavelet-decomposed utilization bands (fast/medium/slow) plus a peakiness score used by the autoscaler. Wavelet decomposition uses a lightweight Haar-ish discrete transform tuned for 1s/10s/60s bands to avoid heavy CPU overhead. 3) Config and rollout: feature-gated behind experimental-reservation-throttle; tunable knobs exposed in Dedicated config (burst_window_ms, peakiness_threshold, soft_throttle_factor, wavelet_bands). 4) Observability: dashboards and burn-down charts added to console for reservation-level throttle events, plus alerts and appetite tests. 5) Tests: unit tests for gate logic, integration tests validating no regression for shared-fleet tenants, and a load canary validating that throttled reservations do not cascade. Checklist: - [x] Design doc linked (Linear ENG-2231) - [x] Unit and integration tests - [x] Telemetry dashboards PR (docs/observability) - [x] Feature gate and rollout plan - [x] Load canary results attached (canary-2026-02-22) Rollout plan: start with 1% of reservations in a single region with telemetry-only enabled, then enable soft-throttle for a week with conservative thresholds before gradual scale to 25% and evaluating eviction rates and tail latency. If tail latencies increase for shared non-reserved tenants, revert soft-throttle and iterate. Related: builds on earlier utilization smoothing work (ENG-2209) and is complementary to global burst-bank refactors.

title: stochastic-fallback-admitter-for-continuous-batcher-starvation-mitigation
preview: Context: Production traffic patterns exposed intermittent starvation of KV-heavy requests in continuous-batch workers when a small number of long-lived KV-pin flows monopolized slots. This caused tail latency spikes under moderate load even though median throughput was fine. Motivation: Introduce a lightweight runtime admission policy that probabilistically relaxes strict slot reservations and induces controlled fallbacks to non-prefetch paths for dominated flows, reducing starvation without regressing steady-state throughput. Design summary: add a StochasticFallbackAdmitter that observes per-slot hold durations and per-flow KV-saturation score, computes a time-decayed weight, and performs a small-probability fallback decision when a scorer exceeds a runtime-configurable threshold. The admitter is integrated into the continuous-batcher admission path with two knobs: fallback_probability_base and aging_half_life_ms. It also supports a strict-mode toggle for canary rollout (strict-mode=false uses probabilistic fallbacks; strict-mode=true is no-op). Key goals: reduce P99 latency for short requests co-located with KV-heavy flows, avoid deterministic preemption that causes jitter, keep overhead <1% CPU on typical workers, and be togglable via runtime flags/feature gates. Checklist: [x] design doc linked (internal: ENG-2345) [x] unit tests for scorer math and config parsing [x] integration test for deterministic behavior under synthetic KV-heavy load [x] perf benchmark harness for mixed workloads [x] rolling canary plan and playbook updates Implementation notes: - New files: serving/batcher/admitters/stochastic_fallback_admitter.{cc,h} and serving/batcher/admitters/README.md describing heuristics; minor changes to serving/batcher/continuous_batcher.cc to invoke the admitter; metrics exported via observability/metrics_registry.cc (stochastic_fallback_evaluations, stochastic_fallback_taken). - KV cache interaction: rather than evicting pins, the admitter requests a fallback path which avoids prefetch/KV-pin optimization for the specific request; this preserves overall cache health while reducing slot hold time. - Kernel-level changes: no kernel changes; we only add admission logic at the batching layer to avoid risky low-level regressions. - Rollout: default disabled; enabled as opt-in on canary fleet, then ramped using rollout heuristics in docs/ops/rollout.md. Commits summary: ["init: implement stochastic fallback admitter skeleton", "impl: scorer + time-decay math and config parsing", "hook: wire admitter into continuous batcher admission path", "metrics: add observability counters and alerting guidance", "test: unit+integration+perf harness updates", "docs: add rollout playbook and feature flag notes"] Changed areas/files: ["serving/batcher/admitters/stochastic_fallback_admitter.cc", "serving/batcher/admitters/stochastic_fallback_admitter.h", "serving/batcher/continuous_batcher.cc", "observability/metrics_registry.cc", "tests/integration/kv_fallback_integration_test.cc", "benchmarks/continuous_batcher/mixed_kv_sweep.yml", "docs/ops/rollout.md"] Tests and performance: on the internal perf harness (mixed 80% short text gen, 20% long KV-pin flows), enabling probabilistic fallbacks at fallback_probability_base=0.05 and aging_half_life_ms=60000 reduced client-observed P99 latency for short requests by ~36% (120ms -> 77ms) while increasing median token CPU by 0.5% and end-to-end throughput change within noise (+0.8%). Tail improvement came with a small increase in observed cache miss-rate for the fallback cohort (expected). CI: linux-unit-tests (pass), integration-kv (pass), benchmark-smoke (pass), lint (pass), security-scan (pass). Review convo highlights: reviewer Samir: "Looks good; can we quantify worst-case extra load on backend KV service when fallback flips many requests?" Author reply: "Bench shows 0.8% throughput delta and cache miss-rate uptick limited to fallback cohort; added grafana dashboard panel and alert guidance in docs/ops/rollout.md." Reviewer Claire: "Please add safety gate so fallback_probability_base cannot exceed 0.2 in production by config schema." Author update: "Added max_allowed_probability cap and schema validation; updated unit tests." Reviewer Elena: "Add note on interaction with canary prefetch warmups and dedicated pools." Author reply: "Documented and added a check in admitter to avoid fallbacks for requests marked as critical-prefetch." Merge: After two approvals and passing CI, PR was squashed and merged to main on 2026-02-25. Rollout: started on 2026-02-26 targeted canary fleet 5% then 25% with careful alerting on cache miss-rate and backend QPS. Post-merge followups: ENG-2560 (observability dashboard tweaks), ENG-2598 (adaptive fallback tuning based on backend QPS), and a planned RFC to explore deterministic-mini-preempt strategy if stochastic approach underperforms in specific tenants.

title: introduce-server-timebox-and-idempotent-cancel-fallbacks-for-streaming-compat
preview: Context: production customers observed cases where long lived streaming sessions (tool calls + structured output) were left in indeterminate states after a client network disconnect or a client-initiated Cancel. Motivation: we need a predictable server-side safety net that bounds resource residency time, ensures tool-call idempotency when retries/resume occur, and provides a clear fallback path (trimmed response + final event) that OpenAI-compatible clients can rely on. This PR implements a server-side streaming timebox (configurable per-route) that gracefully converts oversized/inflight streams into a final 'timeboxed' terminal event, augments client-cancel handling to be idempotent across reconnect/resume windows, and plumbs a compact resume token that includes tool-call idempotency metadata. Summary of changes: 1) stream manager: add configurable timebox_ms and a deterministic finalization path that emits a structured terminal event ("__redwood_timeboxed__") with a compact summary; 2) openai adapter: interpret Cancel events as idempotent markers tied to resume tokens; retries within the resume window will deduplicate tool calls using a request-level id; 3) tooling: map function-call invocations to idempotency keys and persist minimal call metadata to the stream checkpoint to support safe resume; 4) API: extend the OpenAI-compat streaming spec with two optional fields in the final event (timeboxed=true, resume_hint) without breaking existing clients; 5) tests & docs: integration tests for flaky network simulated disconnects and release notes for the behavior and recommended operational knobs. Rollout plan: feature guarded behind a runtime flag and route-level config. Backwards compatibility: default behavior is unchanged unless a route opts-in. Checklist: [x] unit tests for stream finalizer [x] integration test that simulates disconnect + resume [x] docs page and compatibility notes [x] change log entry. Related: ENG-1023 (stream lifecycle SLO), SUP-4572 (customer incident replicant).

title: Introduce stream-proxy: preflight cred-priming, heartbeat publishers, and tri-SDK version bumps
preview: Problem: multiple SDKs independently were doing cred pre-warm, heartbeat, and streamed telemetry which caused duplicated requests, token churn, and mismatched heartbeat intervals across languages. This led to spiky auth calls under bursty workloads and noisy telemetry. Goal: provide a single lightweight in-repo "stream-proxy" primitive that centralizes preflight credential priming, lightweight heartbeat publishers for long-lived streams, and a compact telemetry envelope for streamed metrics. The change harmonizes behavior across Python/TypeScript/Go SDKs, reduces auth thundering, and enables a safer default for session lifetime handling. Summary of changes: - Add a small in-repo stream-proxy component (lib/stream_proxy/) that exposes an opinionated REST-compatible boundary used by SDK adapters. The proxy supports: cred-priming (pre-warm token and short-lived lease), heartbeat publisher (periodic keepalive events with exponential backoff on failure), and streamed_metrics envelope (compact binary/JSON variant selectable by SDK). - SDK adapters: python/ts/go subpackages updated to call the proxy during session open, to subscribe/unsubscribe heartbeat publishers when streams start/stop, and to emit streamed_metrics via the proxy rather than directly to the telemetry backend. - Ergonomics: simplified client API surface for streaming to a single method on the sdk client: start_stream(options) with opt-in advanced hooks for custom heartbeat intervals and preflight scope. This reduces ceremony for the common case. - Backwards compatibility: when the proxy is not available (older deployments), SDKs fall back to previous per-language behavior; we added a detection and a conservative retry/jitter profile so behavior remains stable. - Version bumps: synchronized minor bumps for SDK packages to make the new ergonomics available: python 0.12.4 -> 0.12.5, typescript 1.6.0 -> 1.6.1, go 1.8.2 -> 1.8.3; changelogs included. Migration notes: - Existing consumers need not change immediately. To opt in to the proxy behavior, set client.start_stream({enable_proxy: true}) or set REDWOOD_STREAM_PROXY=1 in environment. - If you manage a Private deployment, see deployments/stream_proxy/README.md for install/compat notes; the proxy is optional but recommended for high-throughput stream workloads. Testing and verification: unit tests for proxy logic, adapter integration tests for Py/TS/Go, and a lightweight throughput canary in perf-canary/stream-proxy-test. We ran canary traffic for 72h across regions (us-west1/eu-west1) with telemetry validated and auth calls reduced by ~65% under synthetic bursts. Rollout: staged rollout on hosted API with feature-flag gate; automatic fallback on proxy error conditions. Why this is different: rather than adding per-SDK heuristics we provide a thin, deployable coordinator that keeps language ergonomics simple while centralizing auth/heartbeat behavior.

title: Introduce contextual cohort synthesis gate with adaptive weighting
preview:
Motivation: We frequently see spurious regression signals when prompt distributions shift subtly (new metadata, truncated contexts, or injected system instructions). This PR introduces a CI/managed-gate that synthesizes cohorts at evaluation time based on semantic context clusters and adaptively reweights alert signals so short-term distribution shifts do not produce noisy failures.

What this PR does: 1) Adds a new gate type 'contextual_cohort_synthesis' that can be plugged into the CI eval pipeline; 2) Implements a lightweight vector-backed cohort builder that groups examples by a configurable semantic window (prompt+metadata embedding); 3) Computes weighted regression scores across synthesized cohorts with an attenuation factor tied to cohort volatility; 4) Exposes config knobs (min_cohort_size, volatility_smoothing, fallback_anchor) and a canary rollout policy; 5) Emits detailed telemetry (per-cohort counts, volatility, score_delta, attenuation_applied) to the eval metrics pipeline for downstream dashboards and alerting.

Checklist: - [x] Implementation with unit tests and integration smoke tests; - [x] CI hook integration and example workflow; - [x] Telemetry and dashboard dashboard-ready metrics names; - [x] Documentation snippet for ops and config; - [x] Backfill script to re-run recent failing prompt-sets through the gate.

Usage: The gate is opt-in via eval.yml: gates:
  - type: contextual_cohort_synthesis
    config:
      enabled: true
      min_cohort_size: 8
      volatility_smoothing: 0.6
      fallback_anchor: historical_baseline
      attenuation_threshold: 0.15

Testing notes: Unit tests cover the cohort builder, volatility calculation and attenuation rules. Integration tests run on a small synthetic prompt-set fixture (tests/fixtures/semantic-shift/*) and CI includes a smoke run against a clipped production sample. Local dev: ./tools/eval_runner --gate contextual_cohort_synthesis --sample tests/fixtures/semantic-shift/sample.json.

Risk & fallback: When enabled without an anchor, the attenuation can hide true regressions; default is disabled and CI templates include explicit opt-in. Canary rollout uses a small-sample flag and auto-fallback to the historical baseline gate when cohort volatility exceeds 0.9.

Related: ENG-4321 (contextual gating), PM-1189 (reduce false positive regression alerts), SUP-2210 (customer reported noisy CI alerts).

title: Enrich on-call diagnostics with automated runbook suggestions and triage helpers
preview: Context: On-call teams often waste critical minutes assembling the same diagnostic surface (pod logs, recent deploys, graph snapshots, SLO deltas) before they can follow runbooks. This PR adds an automated diagnostics gatherer plus a lightweight runbook-suggestion engine to incident-bot so on-call responders get an actionable triage checklist and templated next steps in Slack/PagerDuty threads. Motivation: reduce mean time to acknowledge (MTTA) and mean time to resolution (MTTR) by surfacing high-value signals automatically and recommending runbook steps tailored to the observed signals. Summary of changes: 1) diagnostics worker: new background worker that gathers a bounded set of artifacts (pod logs tails, pod event summaries, recent deploy metadata, SLO deltas from slo-toolkit, topology hints) with configurable TTL and size limits; 2) suggestion engine: rule-based scorer + template library that maps diagnostic signatures to ranked runbook suggestions and immediate mitigation commands; 3) on-call commands: /incident-diagnose which triggers a diagnostics snapshot and posts a suggested runbook checklist back into Slack; 4) triage UI: compact summary card and links to full artifacts (pre-signed URLs) plus confidence score; 5) observability and audit: metrics for diagnoses-per-hour, avg suggestion confidence, and feature flags to enable/disable production use. Compatibility: integrates with existing Slack and PagerDuty hooks, and fetches SLOs using the slo-toolkit API. Security: diagnostics snapshots include redaction hooks and respect KMS-backed secrets; we add an opt-out setting per team. Rollout: feature-gated behind the config flag incident_bot.autorunbook.enabled (default off). Rollout plan includes staged enablement for internal SRE team, canary on a small Slack workspace, and full rollout after 2 weeks of monitoring. Checklist: - [x] Unit tests for scorer heuristics - [x] Integration tests against a local slo-toolkit stub - [x] e2e test that posts to a test Slack workspace - [x] Docs + runbook templates added - [x] Feature flag covered and telemetry emitted Testing notes: use the provided test harness under test/harness/diagnostics to simulate cluster events and SLO regressions. See linear ENG-942 for acceptance criteria and the linked design doc (internal link) for decision rationale. Backwards compatibility: when the feature flag is off, no runtime change; the commands are hidden. Rollback: disable feature flag and revoke pre-signed artifact URLs if necessary.

title: Introduce Signal Preservation Matrix and latency-attribution grok
preview: Context: long-running and async inference pipelines were losing small-but-critical invocation context across stage boundaries (streaming proxies, sidecars, queued workers). This caused noisy alerts, broken token-level latency attribution, and poor rollback signals during incidents. This PR introduces the Signal Preservation Matrix (SPM): a compact, TTL-bound context envelope propagated with spans/metrics/logs; and a latency-attribution grok that maps low-level span phases to token-phase buckets so we can attribute cost/latency consistently across stages. Changes include telemetry enrichment in the serving runtime, an ingestion-time marker that tags preserved signals, a small schema migration for the telemetry index to support SPM keys, three dashboard panels for SPM preservation rate / cross-stage latency-bridge / token-phase waterfall, and an interactive alert-sensitivity tuner that recommends per-route alert thresholds and noise-reduction knobs. Commits: 1) add SPM envelope and propagation hooks to runtime (propagate via headers, span tags, and metadata blobs); 2) ingest-time SPM hydratator and index migration; 3) latency-attribution grok and mapper service with unit tests; 4) dashboard json + widgets and example saved queries; 5) alert-sensitivity tuner backend + UI; 6) docs, migrations, and explainable telemetry-runbook. Impact: staging runs show a 42% reduction in duplicate/noise alerts on busy routes, end-to-end token-phase attribution accuracy improved on sampled traces (measured F1 increase from 0.74 to 0.88), and the additional ingestion CPU cost measured <1.5% at p95 under our staging traffic profile. Backwards compatibility: SPM is opt-in per-route (default off for existing customers) and falls back to current behavior if missing. Testing & CI: added unit tests for propagation and mapper logic, integration tests for enriched traces across a 3-stage async pipeline, and dashboard smoke tests. The first CI run had a lint failure (missing import in the dashboard generator) which was fixed in the second commit; e2e tests passed on the final run. Migration: add migrations/20260220_spm_migration.sql; the migration adds a small index column and a one-time backfill; migration guide included in docs/observability/spm.md. Runbook: brief incident playbook added with steps to toggle SPM per-route, run alert-sensitivity calibration, and roll back the index migration if necessary. Merge: Approved by Diego and Priya after one round of changes; squashed and merged to main. Notes: keep sampling of preserved signals low by default to limit cardinality; the alert-sensitivity tuner will suggest conservative defaults and can be gradually widened by SRE during rollout.

Changes include telemetry enrichment in the serving runtime, an ingestion-time marker that tags preserved signals, a small schema migration for the telemetry index to support SPM keys, three dashboard panels for SPM preservation rate / cross-stage latency-bridge / token-phase waterfall, and an interactive alert-sensitivity tuner that recommends per-route alert thresholds and noise-reduction knobs. Commits: 1) add SPM envelope and propagation hooks to runtime (propagate via headers, span tags, and metadata blobs); 2) ingest-time SPM hydratator and index migration; 3) latency-attribution grok and mapper service with unit tests; 4) dashboard json + widgets and example saved queries; 5) alert-sensitivity tuner backend + UI; 6) docs, migrations, and explainable telemetry-runbook. Impact: staging runs show a 42% reduction in duplicate/noise alerts on busy routes, end-to-end token-phase attribution accuracy improved on sampled traces (measured F1 increase from 0.74 to 0.88), and the additional ingestion CPU cost measured <1.5% at p95 under our staging traffic profile. Backwards compatibility: SPM is opt-in per-route (default off for existing customers) and falls back to current behavior if missing. Testing & CI: added unit tests for propagation and mapper logic, integration tests for enriched traces across a 3-stage async pipeline, and dashboard smoke tests. The first CI run had a lint failure (missing import in the dashboard generator) which was fixed in the second commit; e2e tests passed on the final run. Migration: add migrations/20260220_spm_migration.sql; the migration adds a small index column and a one-time backfill; migration guide included in docs/observability/spm.md. Runbook: brief incident playbook added with steps to toggle SPM per-route, run alert-sensitivity calibration, and roll back the index migration if necessary. Merge: Approved by Diego and Priya after one round of changes; squashed and merged to main. Notes: keep sampling of preserved signals low by default to limit cardinality; the alert-sensitivity tuner will suggest conservative defaults and can be gradually widened by SRE during rollout.

title: Ensure atomic SSE delta emission and robust JSON boundary guards for tool/function call streams
preview:
Motivation: We observed a class of failures in long-running streaming responses where partial JSON objects emitted as SSE 'delta' fragments could be delivered out-of-order or combined with delimiter anomalies after network retries or proxy replays. This manifested as broken tool/function-call payloads, intermittent JSON parse errors in downstream OpenAI-compat clients, and rare duplicate function-sentinel emissions under high-concurrency bursts. The goal of this PR is to make SSE delta emission atomic with explicit boundary guards so clients can deterministically reassemble structured outputs and tool call payloads even under retries or fragment reordering.

Summary of changes: implemented an atomic delta buffer on the SSE emitter that guarantees each logical token/event batch is emitted as an indivisible unit (either fully emitted or retried in full), introduced locked boundary markers around JSON object starts/ends to avoid partial-object exposures, added a lightweight per-event seqid + checksum header to assist client-side resynchronization, hardened the parser reassembly path to prefer checksum-confirmed joins, and added a suite of deterministic integration tests and CI gating.

What this fixes: prevents partial-JSON leak where a truncated object was emitted without its closing brace; eliminates cases where function-call sentinels (e.g., "__CALL_START" / "__CALL_END") could be split across SSE chunks causing the OpenAI-compat layer to misinterpret tool call boundaries; reduces duplicate-sentinel emission by ensuring retries replay whole delta units rather than individual token fragments.

Behavioral notes and compatibility: the emitter adds an additional SSE metadata header (X-Redwood-Seqid and X-Redwood-Checksum) to each delta event. Clients that do not inspect these headers will continue to work unchanged because the payload format is unchanged; clients that adopt the headers can detect out-of-order or partially-applied windows and request deterministic resync. The change preserves existing API contracts and is opt-in from a client-resync perspective, so this is non-breaking.

Testing and validation: added new unit tests for the parser reassembler (tests/sse/test_delimiter_race.py), end-to-end integration tests that fuzz split points across UTF-8 boundaries and function-call sentinels (tests/sse/test_token_ordering_integration.py), and deterministic fixtures under src/runtime/testing/fixtures/sse_partial_json.json. CI pipeline was extended with a gated job (infra/ci/sse_pipeline.yml) that runs the integration suite under multiple network fragmentation patterns. Post-merge load test (internal): 48-hour run simulated 12k concurrent streams with median latency unchanged and visible reduction in parse-error rate from 0.37% to 0.01% on the sampled workload.

Docs and observability: added docs/compat/sse-guidelines.md describing the seqid/checksum model and best practices for client resync. Emitted per-event metrics (pkg/observability/sse_metrics.ts) for delta-retries and checksum-mismatch events to help operators identify degraded client reassembly behavior.

Checklist: - [x] design and API review with API and runtime teams - [x] unit + integration tests added - [x] CI gating for sse integration suite - [x] docs updated - [x] observability hooks added - [x] rolled out to canary region and monitored

Rollout plan: merged to main -> staged canary in eu-west-1 and us-west-2 for 48 hours -> monitor checksum-mismatch and delta-retry metrics -> graduate to global. Controlled fallback: runtime exposes a config flag runtime.sse.atomic_delta=false for emergency rollback.

Related work: references ENG-3912 (flaky function-call parsing incident) and SUP-2213 customer report about partial JSON in streamed tool output. See also prior SSE work (various PRs) for framing history.

Notes on code areas: src/openai_compat/sse/parser.rs: added resync path that uses seqid/checksum, tolerant whitespace handling, and stricter sentinel anchoring. src/runtime/streaming/sse_emitter.cpp: atomic buffer implementation, per-event header encoding, and retry semantics. src/api/handlers/streaming_handler.go: small handler updates to propagate emitter headers through proxy layers. tests/*: deterministic integrations and fuzz harness.

Potential follow-ups: - upstream client library helpers to consume seqid/checksum and offer automatic resync - expand checksums to cover KV cache prefix fragments for cached responses - further reduce allocations in emitter buffer for extreme-scale workloads

This PR was intentionally kept focused on correctness and observability; subsequent PRs will drive micro-optimizations to reduce peak memory from the new buffer under extreme burst patterns.

title: Introduce fast-feedback lanes for trivial changes and a dependency safety gate
preview:
Motivation: CI cycle time has been steadily increasing as we add more integration and security checks. For small, low-risk changes (docs, single-file lint fixes, infra config tweaks) engineers should get feedback in under 5 minutes. At the same time we need an automated, conservative gate for dependency updates to catch transitive supply-chain regressions.

Summary: this PR introduces a small change detection tool and a staged CI workflow that routes PRs to a fast-feedback lane or a full-check lane based on change classification. It also adds a composite dependency-safety action that performs transitive dependency quarantine checks and configurable quarantine policies.

What changed: 1) .github/workflows/staged-ci.yml implements two lanes: fast-path (lint/units/docs + cached build) and full-path (integration, e2e, heavy flakiness-prone tests). 2) tools/change_detector: tiny Rust binary used in the CI to classify PRs by file globs and repo-scope rules. 3) .github/actions/dep-safety: composite action that runs our internal transitive-scan.sh, invokes Snyk for vulnerability context, and can attach a work item to Linear and block merging if policy thresholds are exceeded. 4) CI cache keying updates: finer-grained cache scopes keyed on workspace-subset to reduce invalidation blast radius. 5) docs/ci/fast-feedback.md describing developer experience, how to opt-out, and adding a quickci label to force fast-path or to bypass fast-path when requested.

Checklist: [x] change detector binary + unit tests, [x] staged-ci workflow, [x] dep-safety composite + integration test in CI, [x] docs and READMEs, [x] performance telemetry added to Console for CI latency.

Impact: default is conservative: most PRs still go to full path until the change-detector pool of rules grows. Fast-path is honored only for PRs that touch docs, shaders, README, comments, and single-package lint-only changes. Rolling rollout: feature flag via workflows/ci-config.yml to enable fast-path org-wide in phases.

Related: Linear ENG-15802 (ci-fast-feedback), ENG-16210 (dep-gate), and notes in #infra-ci slack for rollout planning.

How to validate locally: 1) cargo build --manifest-path=tools/change_detector/Cargo.toml 2) tools/change_detector --base main --head HEAD --rules-file .github/ci-rules.yaml 3) run actions locally via act or use GitHub UI to open a PR to a personal branch.

title: fp8-autotune-dequant-rewrite-and-kernel-router
preview:
Motivation: We observed cache-sensitive and sequence-length-dependent regressions when serving FP8/INT8 model variants on the high-throughput path. Two root causes surfaced in profiling: (1) redundant dequantization stubs inserted by the compiler leading to extra memory traffic across the KV cache boundary, and (2) suboptimal kernel selection for mixed FP8/INT8 attention matmuls at mid-sequence lengths, which increases both latency tail and per-token GPU utilization. This PR introduces an autotuned FP8 dequant rewrite pass (dequant-rewriter) plus a scheduler-aware kernel router that picks kernels based on runtime signals (sequence position, KV cache hit pattern, and batching profile).

Summary of changes:
- Implement dequant-rewriter: a graph-level pass that coalesces adjacent dequant ops, hoists dequantization out of hot KV cache paths when safe, and emits a fused "dequant+transpose" kernel where applicable. The pass supports conservative aliasing checks and a feature-flagged faster mode that assumes stable KV shapes in Dedicated deployments.
- FP8 autotuner: offline calibration tool and lightweight runtime probe that chooses between FP8-fused kernels, hybrid FP8/INT8 kernels, or fallback FP16 kernels per model and per-device. Tuner's decisions are persisted in model-serving metadata and can be overridden by rollout flags.
- Kernel router: scheduler integration that consumes continuous-batching statistics (batch size distribution, arrival jitter) and KV cache telemetry to choose kernel variants at request-time. Router minimizes kernel switches within a batch and prefers kernels that maintain low memory traffic across KV boundaries.
- Scheduler hints: expose a small set of hints (seq_pos_bucket, kv_hot_ratio, batch_shape_key) from the serving runtime to the kernel selector. These are lightweight scalar tags attached to batch descriptors.
- Tests and regressions: microbenchmarks for gbench and internal canary; unit tests for the dequant-rewriter; end-to-end perf test in the perf-canary workflow.
- Documentation: notes in docs/serving/quantization.md and a new troubleshooting section describing common FP8 failure modes and mitigation.

Design notes and rationale:
- We avoided aggressive whole-graph FP8 lowering because (a) some models use dynamic dispatch patterns incompatible with a single lowering, and (b) dequantization hoisting across KV cache write-back needs conservative checks to avoid stale keys. The rewriter therefore applies targeted fusion and hoisting, with a safe fallback to existing dequant ops when aliasing cannot be proven.
- Autotuner workflow: an offline pass analyzes representative prompts to build a candidate set. At runtime, a short probe (10s of warmup tokens) validates latency/throughput and writes a per-model best-variant tag. For private and dedicated tenants, the faster autotune mode is enabled by default; hosted tenants require opt-in.

Benchmarks (NVIDIA A100s, sequence mix representative of chat workloads):
- Baseline: mixed FP16 path (pre-change)
  - P50 latency: 86ms
  - P95 latency: 220ms
  - Tokens/sec: 35k
- Post-change (autotuned FP8-fused kernels + dequant-rewriter):
  - P50 latency: 72ms (16% reduction)
  - P95 latency: 165ms (25% reduction)
  - Tokens/sec: 45k (28% increase)
- For small-batch/high-jitter scenarios the kernel router avoided pathological kernel switches and reduced tail variance by ~30%.

Cost impact: in Dedicated benchmarks the improvements correspond to ~22% lower token-GPU-hours for the measured workload mix.

Rollout plan and safety:
- Feature-flagged under runtime.feature.fp8_autotune; default off for hosted mainline for 48h while we collect telemetry.
- Canary deployment to perf-canary and one Dedicated customer (internal) for 72h.
- Automatic rollback policy: if P99 latency increases by >10% or error rate increases by >0.5% over baseline within a 10m window, the rollout controller reverts to the previous kernel tag.

CI and validation:
- Unit and integration tests added under runtime/quant and serving/scheduler.
- Perf canary runs showed initial flakiness due to an aliasing edge-case; fixed by strengthening the alias analysis in the rewriter.
- CI: initial run failed one flaky test (serve_kv_integration_test) and was rerun: final CI passed.

Migration and compatibility notes:
- Models that embed custom kernels may require a one-line model metadata opt-in: "quant.auto_tune: true". We added a compatibility probe that logs recommended kernels without changing runtime behavior until opt-in.
- No API surface change to the Redwood public API.

Observability:
- New metrics: quant_kernel.variant_chosen, quant_autotune.probe_latency, quant_dequant.fused_ops.
- Traces: added tags for seq_pos_bucket and kernel_variant to GPU traces to make regressions easier to root-cause.

Related work: closes ENG-4231 (autotune baseline), progresses ENG-4278 (KV-aware scheduling).

Commits summary: see commits field below.

Files changed (high level): see changed_files field below.

Follow-ups:
- Add FP8 support to the GPU profiler export (ENG-4305).
- Expand autotune dataset to include longer-form decoding patterns.
- Evaluate similar rewriter for INT8-only models where dequantization churn is observed.

title: Add GPU thermal-throttle emulator and warmup stability harness
preview:
Motivation: Intermittent production incidents traced to GPU thermal/power throttling during long inference bursts caused rare regressions in tail latency and kernel scheduling. We need a reproducible, low-overhead emulator to inject thermal/power profiles into benchmark runs and a repeatable warmup-stability harness to measure time-to-steady-state under those conditions. This PR introduces a GPU thermal-throttle emulator and a warmup stability harness that: 1) simulates frequency/power capping curves, 2) injects transient throttles at configurable epochs, 3) coordinates with the existing warmup priming runner to capture time-to-convergence and tail variance, and 4) writes compact stability artifacts for downstream analysis.

What changed (high level): - New thermal_emulator/ module (Python) that runs an in-process state machine describing temperature/power curves and emits a small shim library to throttle CUDA kernel launch pacing via a token-bucket backpressure hook. - warmup_harness/ runner that composes the emulator with multi-sequence primer scenarios and records per-epoch quantiles, KV cache hit rates, and kernel scheduling counters. - reports/stability exporter that generates a single 'stabscore' JSON and a zipped set of perf flamepoints for each run. - CI bench job orchestration added to ci/bench_ci.yaml with a soft-failure rerun for flaky perf thresholds. - docs/thermal_emulator.md with runbook and failure-mode guidance.

Why this is different: Existing harnesses focus on request pattern and KV cache priming; they don't model hardware-level throttles. The emulator is intentionally lightweight (no privileged operations) — it simulates the observable effects (reduced effective throughput, increased per-kernel latency variance) so we can reproduce outages in CI without special infra.

How to run locally: 1) scripts/setup_bench_env.sh --install-deps 2) python -m benchmark_lab.thermal_emulator --profile profiles/cooldown.json & 3) ./scripts/run_warmup_harness.sh --config examples/warmup/heavy_gpu_p95.yaml --output /tmp/stability_run.zip

Checklist: - [x] Implementation of emulator core and token-bucket shim - [x] Warmup harness integration and reporters - [x] Unit tests for emulator state transitions - [x] Small-scale system test run on internal GPU queue (passed) - [x] Documentation and runbook - [x] Bench CI job added (soft-failure enabled)

Performance summary (representative): On the 'heavy_gpu_p95' scenario we saw: baseline stabscore 0.94 -> under simulated throttle 0.72 (regression captured), median latency increase 18%, p99 increase 42%, KV cache priming time increased by 1.7x. Flamepoints attached to report for kernel-level attribution.

Migration notes: No customer-facing API changes. The emulator is opt-in for benchmark runs and disabled by default in nightly pipelines. Recommend adding a dedicated periodic CI job (weekly) that runs the harness against the production model catalog to detect regressions related to thermal sensitivity.

Related work: ENG-2145 (investigate tail incidents), ENG-3301 (warmup priming improvements).

title: Introduce RAG rehearsal sandbox with step-mocks and eval hooks
preview:
Motivation: Add a small, local-first RAG "rehearsal" quickstart that helps engineers and evaluators iterate on end-to-end scenarios deterministically. The sandbox provides: 1) a scenario YAML format for stepwise interactions (queries, tool calls, agent decisions), 2) step-mock harnesses to replace external tools (search, embeddings, vector stores) with canned responses, 3) a lightweight CLI runner to replay scenarios locally and capture structured traces, and 4) eval hooks that emit run-level metrics and Golden-expected assertions for CI integration.

This PR introduces the example project, docs, and a test harness.

Checklist:
- [x] Add quickstart scaffolding and README
- [x] Implement scenario schema and runner CLI
- [x] Provide three sample scenarios (happy path, ambiguous retrieval, safety-fallback)
- [x] Add step-mock implementations for search/knn/embeddings
- [x] Integrate telemetry hooks for trace export (jsonl)
- [x] Wire up a smoke test in CI
- [x] Update quickstarts index and docs sidebar

Usage summary: After checkout run `./bin/rag-rehearse run scenarios/happy-path.yml --mock-store=fixtures/mock_store.json --out=out/happy-path.json`

The runner will output a structured trace of prompt fragments, tool calls, model responses, and evaluation assertions. This makes it easy to reproduce failures reported by customers or caught by automated evals.

Commits:
- initial scaffold: add quickstart directory, README, license note
- add scenario schema and parser
- implement CLI runner and trace exporter
- add three example scenarios and fixtures
- add CI smoke job and docs index entry

Changed files (high level):
- quickstarts/rag-rehearsal-sandbox/README.md
- quickstarts/rag-rehearsal-sandbox/bin/rehearse (executable runner script)
- quickstarts/rag-rehearsal-sandbox/schema/scenario.yaml
- quickstarts/rag-rehearsal-sandbox/scenarios/happy-path.yml
- quickstarts/rag-rehearsal-sandbox/scenarios/ambiguous-retrieval.yml
- quickstarts/rag-rehearsal-sandbox/scenarios/safety-fallback.yml
- quickstarts/rag-rehearsal-sandbox/fixtures/mock_store.json
- quickstarts/rag-rehearsal-sandbox/hooks/telemetry_exporter.py
- docs/quickstarts-index.yml (added entry)

Tests & CI: Added a quick smoke job in .github/workflows/rehearse-smoke.yml which runs the runner against the three included scenarios and asserts that the exported traces contain expected assertion tags. CI runs: lint (pass), unit (pass), smoke (pass).

Review conversation summary:
- Diego: "Nice minimal surface, helps reproduce a lot of postmortems. Could we make the schema optional fields explicit?" Author reply: "Added more explicit field docs and defaults in schema and README (see commit 3)."
- Ava: "CLI UX looks good. Please ensure the mock_store format is compatible with existing vector-store fixtures." Author reply: "Adjusted fixture format to match vector-store shim used in other quickstarts; added conversion helper in fixtures/convert_mock.py."
- Liam: "Telemetry export should be opt-in; don't log PII by default." Author reply: "Telemetry exporter defaults to local jsonl file and strips prompt secrets; added note in README and env var to toggle enrichment."

Additional notes:
- The scenario schema is intentionally compact and opinionated to keep the quickstart approachable; teams can extend it for more complex orchestration.
- The step-mock harness uses a priority-matching rule set so teams can create partial-match fixtures for fuzzier retrieval tests.
- Release notes: "Add RAG rehearsal sandbox quickstart: local runner, scenario schema, step-mocks, and telemetry hooks. Useful for reproducible debugging and automated evals."

Migration/compatibility: "No breaking changes. This is an additive quickstart and does not change runtime or SDK behavior."

title: Proactive mitigations simulation harness and playbook safety gates
preview:
Motivation: On several recent incidents automated mitigations triggered by the incident-bot caused partial service disruption because there was no pre-run simulation of stateful actions and limited safety gating. This PR introduces a lightweight simulation harness that executes mitigation steps against a sandboxed state model and a set of declarative safety gates that must pass before the bot can apply real-world actions.

What this PR does (single-string description): Adds a mitigation simulation harness, a safety-gate DSL for playbooks, a test suite with representative incident scenarios, and small plumbing to record simulation traces in S3 for post-run analysis. Includes documentation, a canary rollout path, CLI helpers (incubate mode) and a feature flag to opt-in per-team.

Checklist: 
- [x] Simulation harness core runtime (deterministic, time-boxed) 
- [x] Safety-gate DSL and evaluator (thresholds, whitelists, dry-run-only exceptions) 
- [x] Unit and integration tests (coverage for 7 common mitigations) 
- [x] CI job adding simulation acceptance tests 
- [x] Documentation and README for playbook authors 
- [x] Opt-in feature flag and incremental rollout plan

Design notes: The harness runs a simulated environment that models key downstream state transitions: service replica counts, leader elections, routing-table updates, and external API rate limits. Mitigation steps implement a small SimulationAdapter interface (simulate(), apply()). Step authors implement simulate() to return a discrete list of state deltas; the harness composes deltas and checks safety gates before returning PASS/FAIL. Safety gates are declarative JSON with rules like max_service_down_time_seconds, max_restarts, and allowed_targets; the evaluator produces a human-readable report.

Backward compatibility: All existing playbooks will continue to work. By default the simulation runs in dry-run and does not block automated mitigations until the feature flag is enabled for a team. Safety gates default to permissive on opt-in, but the admin console will suggest recommended gate values based on historical incident telemetry.

Testing and verification: Added unit tests for gate evaluator and simulation engine. Added integration acceptance tests that run three synthetic incidents: noisy-restart-loop, partial-network-partition, and degraded-cache-eviction. CI runs the acceptance suite under the new workflow. Local repro: ./scripts/run-mitigation-sim.sh --scenario noisy-restart-loop --write-trace ./tmp/trace.json

Commits summary: See commits list below. High level: implement harness, implement gates DSL, wire feature flag, tests, docs, CI.

Changed files (high level): sources: incident-bot/sim/, incident-bot/playbooks/gates/, incident-bot/cli/, docs/playbooks/simulation.md, ci/workflows/sim-acceptance.yml, tests/fixtures/scenarios/*.json.

Rollout plan: Enable feature flag in staging for 2 weeks with trace collection. Run simulated mitigations on nightly canary incidents and review traces. After signoff enable opt-in in production for teams, then progressively require safety gates for fully automated mitigations.

Merge: squash-and-merge after 2 approvals and CI green.

Why this is different: Instead of only gating on simple dry-run checks, the harness executes a compact deterministic simulation of state transitions which catches class of races and mis-ordered steps that previously caused regressions. The design intentionally keeps the simulator conservative (no heavy modeling) to avoid flakiness and keep runs fast (~200–500ms per step in tests).

title: introduce-cost-scribe-routing-and-latency-aware-quant-suggester
preview: Problem: Customers with mixed latency/cost SLOs often leave batching/caching/quantization defaults untouched. That produces suboptimal unit economics for tail workloads and bursty patterns. Motivation: introduce a lightweight routing and suggestion layer (Cost Scribe) that records per-route micro-econ signal, uses a latency-aware quantization suggester to create conservative quant profiles, and surfaces safe defaults in the Console so product engineers can opt-in without manual experimentation. This PR wires three pieces: 1) A routing heuristic that tags requests with a cost_economy score (serving/router/cost_scribe_router.go). The score is computed from token length histogram, recent latency percentiles, and prefill hit rate. 2) A latency-aware quantization suggester prototype (serving/quant/latency_aware_suggester.py) that produces quant profiles (bits/activation scale) with a safety margin tuned to measured tail latencies. 3) Minimal Console UI and docs to show suggested defaults and an opt-in toggle (console/ui/cost-suggestions.md, docs/notes/cost-scribe-rollout.md). Changes are non-breaking; defaults remain unchanged unless the user explicitly applies suggestions. Rollout plan: feature gated under FF_COST_SCRIBE. Start with a 5% shadow routing rollout to collect signals (no active rewrites), then escalate to suggestion-only UI, then automated apply for enterprise Dedicated tenants after manual review. Migration notes: new metrics (cost_scribe_score, suggested_quant_profile) require a short migration of our metrics ingestion; infra config file included. Tests: added unit tests around scoring heuristics and an e2e smoke test to validate the suggestion pipeline. Checklist: - [x] scoring heuristic implemented - [x] quant suggester prototype - [x] tests added - [x] console docs - [x] telemetry config - [x] FF gate added
