title: presubmit-stabilizer-adds-lightweight-toolchain-images-and-targeted-lint-tenancy
preview:
Context and motivation: Presubmit pipelines have grown noisy and slow as more teams enabled heavy linters and end-to-end integration checks. This PR introduces a 'Presubmit Stabilizer'—a set of changes that reduce I/O, shrink toolchain images for fast lanes, and add a tenancy model for linters so teams can opt into expensive checks.

Goals:
- Reduce presubmit wall time for common PRs
- Maintain strict checks for teams that need them via opt-in profiles
- Avoid surprising behavior for contributors (opt-in by default for strict checks)

Summary of changes:
1) ci/toolchain/Dockerfile.slim: new slim toolchain image (Debian-slim base, preinstalled minimal build-toolchain, pip/poetry cache layers).
2) .github/workflows/presubmit.yml: split presubmit lanes into fastpath (uses slim image, minimal devdeps) and strictpath (full image, opt-in via LINT_PROFILE label or PRESUBMIT_LINT_PROFILE env). Added a presubmit stabilization step that snapshots devdeps and computes a lightweight prune plan.
3) ci/presubmit-runner/main.go: small Go-based runner to select lane, warm cache keys, and orchestrate fast-lint adapter.
4) tools/lint/fast-lint-adapter.sh: wrapper that runs only fast linters (format, import-order, simple-typo-check) and annotates results; it also defers heavy analysis to the strict lane.
5) scripts/prune-devdeps.py: heuristic pruner that compares devdeps against a snapshot and removes transitive-only packages during the fastpath container build.
6) docs/ci/presubmit.md: new documentation on LINT_PROFILE, how to opt into strict checks, and recommended developer workflows.

Checklist:
- [x] Add slim toolchain image and CI lanes
- [x] Implement lightweight devdep pruner used during image build
- [x] Provide opt-in lint tenancy and docs
- [x] Add telemetry points (presubmit duration, cache hit/miss)
- [x] Validate against existing large PRs in perf-canary

Motivation details: We observed median presubmit runtimes of ~6:20 and a bimodal tail caused by dev dependency install variability. By moving to a slim image with preinstalled minimal tools and by avoiding full devdep installs on fastpath the median dropped to ~4:10 in internal canaries (~34% improvement).

Design notes:
- Fastpath is the default for most contributors and uses a reproducible cache key format: presubmit/fastpath/<sha-of-snapshot>. Keys are intentionally narrow to avoid cascade cache misses.
- Strictpath is explicit: adding LINT_PROFILE=team-strict or adding the label "run-strict-lint" triggers full checks.
- The pruner is intentionally conservative: it only removes packages not referenced by any top-level devtool and retains an allowlist for known edge-case tools.

Commits (high level):
- f8d3b2a: add slim toolchain Dockerfile and build config
- b12a9e4: split presubmit workflow: fastpath + strictpath
- c21f7d1: implement presubmit-runner and cache warm logic
- d44e6f0: add fast-lint-adapter and annotations
- e98b0f2: add devdep pruner script and integration test scaffolding
- fa2c11b: docs and telemetry hooks

Files changed (high level): [".github/workflows/presubmit.yml", "ci/toolchain/Dockerfile.slim", "ci/presubmit-runner/main.go", "tools/lint/fast-lint-adapter.sh", "scripts/prune-devdeps.py", "docs/ci/presubmit.md", "ci/build-image/Makefile", "ci/containers/README.md", "ci/cache/keying.md", "observability/metrics/presubmit_metrics.yaml", "tests/presubmit/integration/fastpath_test.sh", "tests/presubmit/integration/strictpath_test.sh", "tools/annotators/gh-annotate.py", "Dockerfile.full"]

Review conversation summary:
- Nate Fischer (reviewer): Requested to make the cache key format stable across repo moves; asked for an example key and a brief explanation in docs. Response: Updated docs/ci/presubmit.md with the keying spec and added a helper shell function in ci/build-image/Makefile to derive keys.
- Priya Desai: Raised concern that pruning could remove packages needed by rarely-run scripts. Requested an allowlist and integration tests. Response: Added allowlist support (ci/toolchain/pruner_allowlist.txt) and two fast integration tests under tests/presubmit/integration. Priya re-reviewed and approved.
- Liam O'Neill: Asked for telemetry hooks to signal false-negatives when fast-lint skips checks. Response: Added presubmit_metrics.yaml and wired basic counters in presubmit-runner; also added an alert in observability to catch delta regressions >5% over baseline.

CI runs:
- Initial run: lint failed due to shellcheck strictness; fixed via fast-lint-adapter.sh update.
- Second run: integration test flake (network timing), rerun and passed.
- Final run: all checks green.

Merge notes: Squash merge performed after approvals from Nate and Priya and green CI. Merged commit: "squash: presubmit stabilizer: slim images, pruner, fast-lint, split lanes" (merge commit sha: abcdef1234567890).

Post-merge actions:
- Released slim image to internal registry tag: redwood/presubmit-toolchain:slim-20260222
- Notified platform teams via #infra-ci
- Scheduled a 1-week canary to monitor presubmit durations and false-negative telemetry

Known limitations and follow-ups:
- Some legacy repos rely on global devdeps; next work item (ENG-2054) will add repository-level opt-out and a mitigation shim.
- The pruner allowlist will need periodic maintenance; added weekly job to refresh candidate removals.
- There's a small risk that a rare static-analysis check is missed; strictpath remains available and recommended for core infra repos.

Conclusion: This PR reduces CI cost and developer feedback loops by favoring a fast default path while preserving strict checks for teams that require them. The change is opt-in for strict checks and includes docs, tests, and telemetry to guard regressions.

title: introduce-ecohistorian-batch-indexer-and-defaults-generator
preview:
Motivation: We repeatedly see suboptimal tenant defaults for batching and cache sizing because current heuristics don't leverage historical batch shapes and cross-tenant cache hit signals. This PR adds an offline "ecohistorian" indexer service that ingests anonymized serving telemetry and produces tenant-specific candidate defaults (batch shape templates, KV cache size suggestions, and conservative quantization hints). The goal is automation: feed the optimizer with statistically grounded defaults instead of hand-tuned heuristics.

What this PR does (summary): 1) New service pkg/ecohistorian: a lightweight indexer that summarizes sequence-length distributions, inter-request spacing, prefix reuse, and cache hit rates into compact signatures. 2) Generator module in tools/econ-defaults: consumes historian signatures to output tenant defaults as YAML/JSON (batching.default, cache.size_hint, quant.profile_candidates). 3) New CLI: `redwood econ-historian import --source <s3/snapshot>` to run the indexer on historical telemetry snapshots and produce a preview diff against current defaults. 4) API: a new GET /internal/econ/defaults-preview endpoint (auth gated) to preview suggested defaults for a tenant. 5) Telemetry: added metrics (ecohistorian.jobs, econ.defaults_suggested, econ.preview_delta_tokens_saved_estimate) and a small dashboard JSON for Console. 6) Tests and CI: unit tests for the indexing pipeline and an integration smoke test that runs the generator on a synthetic snapshot.

Design notes and safety: suggestions are conservative by default. Quant profiles are proposed with an explicit safety score; the generator never emits an aggressive quantization candidate without a fallback path. Suggested defaults include controlled fallbacks: when a suggested quantization is chosen, the system will route a configurable percentage of traffic to a non-quantized variant for 24 hours to detect regressions. The preview endpoint returns both the candidate and the safety metadata.

title: introduce-budget-mapper-mini-batcher-defaults-and-lightweight-quant-profiles
preview:
Motivation: many tenants operate with low to moderate QPS where full tenant-wide prefill and large batch policies add cost without improving tail latency. This PR adds a lightweight 'budget mapper' that emits per-tenant mini-batcher defaults and an opinionated set of small quantization profiles so teams can get cost-down defaults that are safe for production.

Summary of changes: adds a BudgetMapper component that infers conservative mini-batching and cache window defaults from historical unit-econ signals (p95 latency, tokens-per-request, cold-start rate). Introduces a tiny, validated quant_profiles.yaml with three compact profiles (tiny-int8, lean-int4, compat-fp16) and a CLI subcommand 'redwood econ inspect' to preview tenant defaults. Defaults are intentionally conservative and include automatic safe fallbacks (no quantization for models flagged as unsafe). The PR wires telemetry (econ.unit_price_estimate, batching.shape_hint, quant.profile_applied) and a lightweight e2e smoke test that validates latency and token-cost regressions against a golden workload.

Design notes: BudgetMapper is implemented as a single-file controller (pkg/econ/budget_mapper.go) that consumes aggregated profile snapshots (already emitted by existing telemetry) and runs a deterministic rule set to produce config suggestions. The decision graph is intentionally small so it can be audited (no ML training). Mini-batcher defaults focus on microbatch_size, per-tenant prefix_cache_seconds, and warmup_window_ms. Quant suggestion heuristics prefer lean-int4 for non-sensitive models with median request length < 256 and token-efficiency > 0.8.

Testing & safety: unit tests for decision branches, CI smoke (ci/econ-smoke.yml) against a local mock runtime, and an e2e harness that replays a sampled tenant workload to assert no >5% regression in p95 latency and at least 8% reduction in measured token cost for qualifying tenants. We also added a feature flag (feature.econ.budget_mapper.enabled) and rollout steps in the console so operators can opt-in per-tenant.

Migration / rollout: feature gated behind console flag and an opt-in CLI. Default remains off for existing tenants. Operators can run 'redwood econ inspect --tenant TID --dry-run' to preview suggested defaults without applying them. If applied, changes are stored as tenant overrides and can be rolled back with 'redwood econ rollback'.

Checklist:
- [x] BudgetMapper core implementation
- [x] quant_profiles.yaml (three profiles) and validation schema
- [x] CLI preview and apply/rollback helpers
- [x] telemetry hooks and unit-econ metrics
- [x] unit tests and e2e smoke
- [x] docs (observability/econ-telemetry.md)

Related: ENG-3124 contains the decision criteria; PM-198 is the product brief for conservative defaults. SUP-411 was consulted for tenant opt-in UX during an earlier support escalation.

title: quickcheck: add lightweight oncall quick-check CLI and compact diagnostic packer
preview: Implements a small, dependency-light quick-check CLI and a compact diagnostic packer that incident responders can run from the incident channel or a runbook step. Motivation: many oncall incidents are prolonged by manual ad-hoc data collection and noisy attachments. This PR provides a safe, opinionated tool that captures a bounded set of artifacts (sanitized logs, pod status, recent traces, KV cache hints) and emits a single compressed artifact suitable for attaching to an incident thread or uploading to the internal diagnostics bucket. Key properties: fast (<= 15s typical), bounded output size (configurable default 8MB), privacy-first sanitization hooks, dry-run mode, and automatic S3-compatible upload with short-lived presigned URLs. Includes a new slash-command integration for incident-bot (/quickcheck) to trigger the CLI remotely via action runners and a small Webhook consumer used by private deployments. See linked Linear tickets ENG-3267 and ENG-3312 for rollout plan. Checklist: [x] Quick-check CLI implementation, [x] packer sanitizers and size enforcement, [x] slash-command wiring and permission checks, [x] small tutorial in docs/runbooks/quickcheck.md, [x] e2e smoke test harness, [ ] optional telemetry opt-in toggle (follow-up).

title: Sync SDKs: auth/retry presets, stream ergonomics, and minor version bumps
preview: Motivation: Multiple product teams reported subtle differences in default auth token refresh timing and retry jitter across SDKs which led to uneven retry storms during rollout tests. This PR unifies a compact credential adapter, aligns retry presets to latency-aware defaults, and polishes streaming ergonomics (pausable iterators / explicit resume API) across Python/TypeScript/Go language bindings. Summary of changes: - Introduce 'cred-compact' adapter used by all SDKs: smaller surface for users who only need default token refresh+prefetch. - Standardize retry presets: default backoff = exponential base 200ms, max backoff 5s, add latency-aware variant that reduces base to 50ms for sub-50ms SLO routes. - Streaming ergonomics: add Iterator.pause()/resume() semantics (py: AsyncIterator.pause_resume; ts: AsyncGeneratorController; go: StreamHandle.Pause/Resume). - Improve typed errors on stream termination (StreamCancelled, AuthRefreshFailed, BackoffExceeded). - Add telemetry tags for credential policy and retry_preset to events emitted by SDKs. - Bump minimum SDK versions and update changelogs across repos. Checklist: - [x] Design doc linked and reviewed - [x] Cross-language test harness updated - [x] Unit + integration tests added - [x] Docs snippets and examples updated - [x] Release notes drafted Implementation notes: This PR is a coordination patch in the redwood repository that updates language-agnostic design artifacts plus the CI-driven sync spec used by the three SDK repos. The actual SDK repo patches were generated by the repo tooling and are included as submodule-like artifacts under /sdks/sync-patches/ with per-language diffs applied by the release pipeline. Rationale: keeping the canonical coordination surface in redwood (instead of multiple independent PRs) ensures identical defaults and simplifies rollout testing. Backwards compatibility: pause/resume on streams is additive; the compact credential adapter is opt-in for customers who want lower auth ceremony; retry defaults changed only for the nominal preset; we left legacy presets intact for customers relying on older behavior. See release notes for migration guidance.

title: Attenuated warm-pool smoothing and utilization exposer for dedicated fleets
preview: Context: Dedicated fleet warm pools are currently susceptible to short-lived microbursts: a handful of tenants generate transient spikes that cause the warm-pool controller to over-allocate headroom, inflate cost, and produce noisy per-tenant utilization metrics. This PR introduces an attenuated smoothing policy for warm-pool sizing and a lightweight utilization exposer that decouples instantaneous spikes from the long-running reservation logic. Motivation: reduce oscillation-driven overprovisioning, improve stability for tenant SLAs, and provide clearer telemetry to platform teams so they can tune reservations and burst contracts. Changes in this PR (high level): - Introduce an attenuated smoothing window with configurable decay factors and min/max headroom bounds. The algorithm applies exponential decay to short spikes while allowing sustained demand to grow warm pool capacity. - Add microburst detector that can tag allocations as transient (microburst) vs sustained; microburst signals are retained short-term and influence only ephemeral warm allocations. - Expose per-tenant utilization summaries (P50/P95/P99 token throughput and burst incidence rate) to telemetry pipeline with a compact protobuf payload for ingestion. - Split warm-pool decision path: "elastic" (longer smoothing) vs "ephemeral" (microburst buffer) to keep committed reservations stable. - Add feature flag and runtime knobs: warm_pool.smoothing.enabled, warm_pool.smoothing.decay_tau_ms, warm_pool.ephemeral_budget_pct, telemetry.util_bucket_window_ms. Checklist: - [x] Core algorithm and controller changes implemented - [x] Unit tests for smoothing kernel and microburst tagging - [x] Integration test that simulates mixed sustained + bursty tenants and validates headroom change bounds - [x] Telemetry schema and exporter implementation - [x] Docs: operator guide and runtime knobs - [x] Feature-flag gating for staged rollout Commits (summary): - init: add smoothing kernel and interfaces (pkg/autoscaler/smoothing.go) - controller: wire smoothing into warm-pool reconciler and split decision path (pkg/autoscaler/controller.go) - telemetry: add utilization exposer and protobuf (internal/metrics/utilization_exposer.go, proto/utilization.proto) - exporter: push compact metrics to metrics pipeline with sampling (cmd/dedicated/telemetry/exporter.go) - tests: unit and integration harness (tests/integration/warm_pool_smoothing_test.go) - docs: operational guide and examples (docs/operational/warm-pool.md) Files changed (high-level): pkg/autoscaler/smoothing.go, pkg/autoscaler/controller.go, internal/metrics/utilization_exposer.go, proto/utilization.proto, cmd/dedicated/telemetry/exporter.go, docs/operational/warm-pool.md Why this is safe: - Backwards compatible: smoothing is behind warm_pool.smoothing.enabled (default=false for initial rollout). - Telemetry schema is additive; consumers that don't parse the new proto will ignore the new field. - Behavior can be reverted by toggling feature flag or adjusting decay_tau_ms to a neutral value. Testing notes: - Local integration harness simulates 100 tenants with 10% producing 2s microbursts and confirms <7% warm-pool capacity change compared to baseline. - Unit tests cover edge cases including zero traffic, sustained ramp, and pathological bursts. Review conversation summary: - Ethan: requested clearer bounds on min/max headroom and unit for decay_tau_ms. I added validation and docs explaining units are milliseconds and provided reasonable defaults (decay_tau_ms=5000ms, min_headroom=1, max_headroom=5). - Miguel: asked for an additional metric to report number of microburst-tagged allocations per minute; added microburst_alloc_count and unit test. - Ava: suggested moving the telemetry exporter to a separate goroutine to avoid blocking reconciler; refactored exporter to use buffered channel and background worker and added backpressure handling. CI / checks: - CI run 1: go vet + unit tests (flaky DB connection in test harness) -> failed; reran. - CI run 2: full unit + integration (short) -> pass. Observability: - dashboards: added warm-pool smoothing dashboard panel (docs/operational/warm-pool.md) showing headroom, microburst rate, and utilization percentiles. - Alerts: recommend adding alert warm_pool.unexpected_growth if headroom increases >50% over 5m window. Migration/rollout plan: - Stage 1 (dev): enable smoothing for dev clusters for 48h; monitor microburst_alloc_count and headroom trend. - Stage 2 (staging): enable for 1 week, bounded by feature-flag autoswitch if warm_pool.unexpected_growth fires. - Stage 3 (canary->prod): gradual ramp to 10% fleets, then 30%, with automated rollback criteria. Merge outcome: Squash-merged into main after approvals and green CI. Merge commit: abc123def4567890. Release notes: Added attenuated warm-pool smoothing to reduce reactive overprovisioning from short-lived tenant microbursts; introduced per-tenant utilization exposer (P50/P95/P99 + microburst incidence); feature flag warm_pool.smoothing.enabled controls rollout. Followups / TODOs: - ENG-6012: surface per-tenant utilization in Console route. - ENG-5687: tune default decay_tau_ms via A/B tests across dev fleets.
